topics.title: Construction Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET604 - CONSTRUCTION PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Construction Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Construction Project (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *Construction Project* is a **temporary endeavour** undertaken to create a **unique product, service, or result** — specifically, a built facility (building, infrastructure, or industrial asset) — having a definite **beginning** and **end**, defined **scope**, allocated **resources**, and specific **performance objectives** relating to quality, cost, time, and safety.

In the context of KTU's *PBCET604 – Construction Project Management* syllabus, the term "construction project" is interpreted in line with **PMBOK® (Project Management Body of Knowledge)** terminology as adapted to the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Tagging (CO1 – Module 1):**
> After studying this topic, the student should be able to **explain the characteristics, classification, life-cycle, and stakeholder structure of construction projects**, forming the foundational vocabulary used in every subsequent module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Construction Project like baking a wedding cake for a specific client.**
>
> - The **wedding cake** = the *unique deliverable* (no two cakes are identical).
> - The **recipe (drawings + BOQ)** = the *project scope & design*.
> - The **baker, decorator, supplier (flour, sugar)** = the *project team & supply chain*.
> - The **wedding date** = the *project deadline*.
> - The **budget the client gave you** = the *project cost baseline*.
> - **Wedding over → cake is consumed, kitchen is cleaned** = the *project ends* (temporary, not a continuous operation).
>
> Just as a baker cannot extend the wedding date indefinitely or substitute ingredients randomly without failing, a construction manager must hit the **triple constraint of Scope, Time, and Cost** while maintaining **Quality and Safety**.

## 1.3 Why "Construction Projects" is the First Topic

Every other module in PBCET604 (Planning, Scheduling, Resource Management, Risk, Quality, Safety, Contracts) is built on the foundation of understanding **what a construction project is**, **who is involved**, and **how it evolves from idea to handover**. Without this vocabulary, scheduling a CPM network or interpreting a FIDIC clause becomes impossible.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Units in Construction Projects

| Metric | Standard Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Project Cost | ₹ Crore / ₹ Lakh / US\$ million | Financial scale |
| Duration | Months / Weeks (sometimes man-hours) | Time scale |
| Built-up Area | Square Metres (m²) or Square Feet (sft) | Size of building |
| Length of road/pipe | Kilometres (km) | Linear projects |
| Floor Area Ratio (FAR / FSI) | Ratio (dimensionless) | Density regulation |
| Project Complexity Index | Score (0–100) | Risk & planning depth |

> [!NOTE]
> In India, **built-up area is typically quoted in square feet (sft)** in private practice, but **square metres (m²)** is the statutory unit under RERA, NBC 2016, and KMBR (Kerala Municipal Building Rules). Always clarify the unit before cost estimation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content

## 2.1 The Five Defining Characteristics of a Construction Project

A construction project is differentiated from routine construction *operations* (e.g., annual maintenance of a building) by these **five universal characteristics** (PMBOK-aligned):

1. **Temporary Nature (Definitive Start & End)**
   - Every project has a clear start date (e.g., date of agreement / LoI) and an end date (e.g., handing over / Defects Liability Period end).
   - *Why it matters:* Defines the planning horizon and resource commitment.

2. **Unique Deliverable (One-of-a-kind Product)**
   - Even if two buildings look identical, they differ in **location, soil, weather, labour, client, and design intent**.
   - *Why it matters:* Mass-production techniques cannot be used — every project needs custom planning.

3. **Progressive Elaboration (Incremental Definition)**
   - Scope becomes clearer as the project advances — concept → detailed design → construction → commissioning.
   - *Why it matters:* Risk and information uncertainty decrease as the project matures.

4. **Resource-Constrained**
   - Limited **money, men, materials, machinery, and time (5M's)**.
   - *Why it matters:* Trade-offs among the **triple constraints** are inevitable.

5. **Stakeholder-Driven**
   - Multiple parties (owner, designer, contractor, regulator, public) with often conflicting objectives.
   - *Why it matters:* Effective *stakeholder management* is as critical as technical skill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:**
> If asked *"Why is construction management complex?"* — always answer by linking back to the **5 characteristics** above, particularly *uniqueness, temporary nature, and stakeholder multiplicity*.

## 2.2 Classification of Construction Projects (KTU High-Yield)

Construction projects are classified in **four standard ways**. This is a frequently asked 7–14 mark question.

### A. By Nature of Work (Most Common Classification)

| Category | Examples | Typical Owner | Funding Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Building Construction** | Residential, commercial, institutional (schools, hospitals) | Private / Government | Private equity, bank loans, public budget |
| **Infrastructure / Heavy Civil** | Roads, bridges, airports, metros, dams, water supply | Government / PSU | Tax revenue, multilateral loans (ADB, World Bank) |
| **Industrial Construction** | Power plants, refineries, factories, steel plants | Private corporates / PSUs | Corporate CAPEX, project finance |
| **Specialty Construction** | Piling, electrical substations, HVAC, fire-fighting | Sub-contracted | Part of main project cost |

### B. By Sector Ownership

- **Public Sector Projects** — funded by government, governed by *GFC (General Financial Rules)*, *CPWD manuals*, *Kerala PWD*, *CPWD-DSR rates*, e-tendering through *GeM / CPP / Kerala e-tenders*.
- **Private Sector Projects** — funded by private owners/developers, governed by *RERA 2016*, contractual freedom within law.
- **Public-Private Partnership (PPP)** — hybrid; e.g., **Kochi Metro, Smart City Kochi, Cochin International Airport (CIAL)**.

### C. By Delivery / Contract Method

- **Design-Bid-Build (DBB)** — traditional, sequential.
- **Design-Build (DB / EPC)** — single point of responsibility.
- **Turnkey / EPCM** — contractor executes till commissioning.
- **PPP / BOT / BOOT** — long-term concession (15–30 years).

### D. By Size and Complexity

| Size | Approx. Cost | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Small** | < ₹ 1 Crore | House, small shop |
| **Medium** | ₹ 1 – 50 Crore | Apartment complex, school |
| **Large** | ₹ 50 – 500 Crore | Hospital, IT park, flyover |
| **Mega** | > ₹ 500 Crore | Metro corridor, smart city, airport |

## 2.3 Stakeholders in a Construction Project (Analytical Core)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — "Who is a Stakeholder?"**
> Anyone who **affects, or is affected by, the project** is a stakeholder. They are classified as:
> - **Internal** (part of project team) and
> - **External** (outside the project team).

| Stakeholder | Role | Primary Interest |
|---|---|---|
| **Owner / Client** | Initiates & funds the project | Functionality, cost, ROI |
| **Project Management Consultant (PMC)** | Owner's representative | Schedule, quality, cost control |
| **Design Consultant (Architect + Structural + MEP)** | Converts idea into drawings | Design integrity, code compliance |
| **Contractor (Main)** | Executes construction | Profit, safety, timely payment |
| **Sub-contractors** | Specialised work (plumbing, electrical) | Scope clarity, payment |
| **Suppliers / Vendors** | Materials, machinery | Order clarity, payment terms |
| **Regulatory Authorities** (Panchayat / Municipality / PWD / Pollution Control Board) | Approvals & compliance | Statutory adherence |
| **End Users** (e.g., public, occupants) | Use the finished facility | Quality, safety, usability |
| **Financiers / Banks** | Provide loans | Repayment, collateral |
| **Local Community & Environment** | Neighbours, eco-system | Pollution, traffic, displacement |
| **Insurance & Legal Advisors** | Risk transfer & contract vetting | Compliance, claim protection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Listing only "Owner, Contractor, Engineer." KTU examiners expect a **minimum 8–10 stakeholders** mapped to their *interest*, *influence*, and *engagement strategy*. This is a 7-mark sub-question.

## 2.4 The Construction Project Life Cycle (CPLC)

> [!NOTE]
> **CPLC** is the *backbone* of Module 1. Every other module (planning, scheduling, resource management) refers to a *phase* of this life cycle.

The four generic phases are:

1. **Conceptual / Feasibility Phase**
   - Idea → feasibility study → cost-benefit analysis → *go / no-go* decision.
   - Output: **Feasibility Report + DPR (Detailed Project Report)**.

2. **Planning & Design Phase (Pre-construction)**
   - Site investigation, surveys, soil testing, conceptual design, statutory approvals, detailed design, tender documents.
   - Output: **Approved drawings + Bill of Quantities (BOQ) + Tender**.

3. **Execution / Construction Phase**
   - Mobilisation, site clearing, substructure, superstructure, services, finishing, testing.
   - Output: **Physical built facility**.
   - This phase consumes **80–90% of total project cost** — hence most management focus.

4. **Closure / Handover / Operation Phase**
   - Testing & commissioning, snagging, training, handover, *Defects Liability Period* (typically 12–24 months), operation & maintenance begins.
   - Output: **Project closure report + as-built drawings + warranties**.

### KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Stake × Phase Matrix

| Stakeholder | Concept | Planning | Execution | Closure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Owner | Decides | Approves | Monitors | Operates |
| Consultant | Advises | Designs | Supervises | Certifies |
| Contractor | — | Tenders | Builds | Demobilises |
| User | — | Briefs | Inspects | Uses |

## 2.5 Project Success Criteria (KTU "Iron Triangle" + Modern View)

The traditional **Iron Triangle** of project success is:

$$\boxed{\text{Success} \;=\; f(\text{Scope},\;\text{Time},\;\text{Cost},\;\text{Quality})}$$

Modern construction project management (and KTU Module 5) extends this to include:

$$\text{Success} = \underbrace{\text{Scope + Time + Cost + Quality}}_{\text{Iron Triangle}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Safety + Environment + Stakeholder Satisfaction}}_{\text{Modern Pillars}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainability Add-on (NEP 2020 flavour):**
> KTU 2024 emphasises **Green Building (IGBC / GRIHA)** and **carbon footprint reduction**. Be ready to add **Sustainability** as a fifth pillar.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **For Civil Engineers:** Selecting correct contract type for a given project class.
- **For Project Managers:** Building the right team and stakeholder engagement plan.
- **For Quantity Surveyors:** Deciding whether to use *item-rate, lump-sum, or cost-plus* contracts.
- **For Site Engineers:** Understanding *which life-cycle phase* they are in determines their KPIs (e.g., during execution → daily progress; during closure → snagging list).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparative Matrices

> [!NOTE]
> Construction Project Management is a *qualitative-quantitative hybrid* domain. The "derivations" here are *analytical breakdowns* — the rigorous, examiner-pleasing way of presenting a concept.

## 3.1 Derivation-Style Breakdown: From "Idea" to "Handover"

We model a construction project as a **state machine** transitioning through 4 phases. Let the project state be $S_t$ at time $t$.

$$S_t \in \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$$

Where:
- $S_1$ = Conceptual / Feasibility
- $S_2$ = Planning & Design
- $S_3$ = Execution
- $S_4$ = Closure & Handover

The transition function is:

$$T : S_i \;\longrightarrow\; S_{i+1} \quad \text{subject to a Gate-Criterion } G_i$$

### Step-by-Step Transition Logic

**Step 1 — Transition $S_1 \to S_2$ (Concept → Planning)**

The *Gate Criterion* $G_1$ is:

$$G_1 = \text{Feasibility Cleared} \;\land\; \text{Funding Sanctioned} \;\land\; \text{Land Acquired}$$

*Explanation in plain English:* The project moves from idea-stage to design-stage **only after** it is *technically feasible, financially sanctioned, and legally accessible*.

**Step 2 — Transition $S_2 \to S_3$ (Planning → Execution)**

The *Gate Criterion* $G_2$ is:

$$G_2 = \text{Design Approved} \;\land\; \text{Tender Awarded} \;\land\; \text{Statutory Permits Obtained} \;\land\; \text{Work Order Issued}$$

**Step 3 — Transition $S_3 \to S_4$ (Execution → Closure)**

The *Gate Criterion* $G_3$ is:

$$G_3 = \text{Physical Progress} = 100\% \;\land\; \text{Tests Passed} \;\land\; \text{Snag List Closed} \;\land\; \text{Taking-Over Certificate Issued}$$

**Step 4 — Project Closure**

$$G_4 = \text{DLP Period Completed} \;\land\; \text{Performance Certificate Issued} \;\land\; \text{Final Payment Released}$$

### Consolidated Phase–Cost–Stakeholder Model

| Phase | Duration (% of Total) | Cost (% of Total) | Risk Level | Dominant Stakeholder |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ Concept | 5–10% | 1–3% | High (go/no-go) | Owner |
| $S_2$ Planning | 15–25% | 5–10% | Medium-High | Consultant |
| $S_3$ Execution | 50–70% | **80–90%** | **Highest** | Contractor |
| $S_4$ Closure | 5–10% | 2–5% | Low-Medium | Owner + PMC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **execution phase $S_3$** is the single most critical phase because it consumes the bulk of cost and carries the highest accident/quality risk. This is why *scheduling, monitoring, and resource management* modules dominate PBCET604.

## 3.2 Comparative Matrix: Construction vs Manufacturing Projects

This is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU exams.

| Parameter | Construction Project | Manufacturing Project |
|---|---|---|
| Output | One-of-a-kind, site-specific | Repetitive, identical units |
| Location | Site-based, outdoor | Factory-based, indoor |
| Labour | Skilled + unskilled, migratory | Stable, trained workforce |
| Weather dependency | High | Negligible |
| Design changes | Frequent | Rare after tooling |
| Duration | Months to years | Continuous |
| Cost estimation accuracy | $\pm$ 10–30% | $\pm$ 2–5% |
| Safety risk | High (heights, machinery) | Lower (controlled) |
| Stakeholders | Many, dispersed | Fewer, internalised |
| Standardisation | Low | High |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner tip:** When asked to differentiate, present a *table* with at least **6 parameters** and *one-sentence justification* per row. This scores full marks versus a plain paragraph.

## 3.3 Stakeholder Power–Interest Matrix (Mendelow's Adaptation)

A KTU-favourite analytical model. Stakeholders are placed on a **Power × Interest grid**:

$$\text{Engagement Strategy} = f(\text{Power},\;\text{Interest})$$

| Power $\downarrow$ \ Interest $\rightarrow$ | Low Interest | High Interest |
|---|---|---|
| **High Power** | Keep Satisfied (e.g., Senior Govt. Official) | **Manage Closely** (e.g., Owner, Financier) |
| **Low Power** | Monitor (e.g., Local vendors) | Keep Informed (e.g., End users, Community) |

This matrix directly informs the **Stakeholder Engagement Plan** that is a deliverable of the *Project Management Plan* in PMBOK.

## 3.4 Quantitative Example — Cost Distribution Across Phases

Suppose a project has a total budget $C_T = \text{₹ } 100$ Crore. Using typical phase-cost percentages:

$$C_{S_1} = 0.02 \times C_T = \text{₹ } 2.00\;\text{Crore}$$

$$C_{S_2} = 0.08 \times C_T = \text{₹ } 8.00\;\text{Crore}$$

$$C_{S_3} = 0.85 \times C_T = \text{₹ } 85.00\;\text{Crore}$$

$$C_{S_4} = 0.05 \times C_T = \text{₹ } 5.00\;\text{Crore}$$

**Verification (sum should equal $C_T$):**

$$C_{S_1} + C_{S_2} + C_{S_3} + C_{S_4} \;=\; 2.00 + 8.00 + 85.00 + 5.00 \;=\; \text{₹ } 100.00\;\text{Crore}\;\checkmark$$

This visually demonstrates why *cost control during execution* is the most leveraged management activity — a 5% saving in $S_3$ saves **₹ 4.25 Crore**, versus ₹ 0.10 Crore in $S_1$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Phase 1 Conceptual and Feasibility] -- Feasibility Report and Funding --> P2[Phase 2 Planning and Design]

    P4 -- DLP Completion --> P5[Project End]
    P1 -. Risk High Cost 2 Percent .-> P1
    P2 -. Risk Medium Cost 8 Percent .-> P2
    P3 -. Risk Highest Cost 85 Percent .-> P3
    P4 -. Risk Low Cost 5 Percent .-> P4
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Information Flow in a Construction Project

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN1[Owner Brief] --> BL1[Feasibility and DPR]
    BL1 --> IN2[Concept Design]
    IN2 --> BL2[Statutory Approvals]

    IN3 --> BL3[Tender and Award]
    BL3 --> IN4[Construction Programme]
    IN4 --> BL4[Site Execution and Monitoring]
    BL4 --> IN5[Progress Reports and QA QC]
    IN5 --> BL5[Testing and Snagging]
    BL5 --> IN6[Handover and DLP]
    IN6 --> OUT[Project Closure and As Builts]
    BL1 --> FEED1[Feedback Loop 1 Scope Refinement]
    FEED1 --> IN2
    BL4 --> FEED2[Feedback Loop 2 Design Changes]
    FEED2 --> IN3
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a construction project. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from routine construction operations.**

**Model Answer:**

> A *construction project* is a **temporary endeavour** undertaken to create a **unique built facility** with defined scope, cost, time, and quality objectives.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Temporary** — finite start and end date.
2. **Unique deliverable** — no two sites, soil conditions, or clients are identical.
3. **Progressive elaboration** — scope is refined phase by phase.
4. **Resource-constrained & stakeholder-driven** — limited 5M's and multiple parties with conflicting interests.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for characteristics)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a *public sector* and a *PPP* construction project with one suitable example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Public Sector | Public-Private Partnership (PPP) |
|---|---|---|
| Funding | Fully government (tax revenue) | Shared between government and private party |
| Risk | Entirely on government | Distributed via concession agreement |
| Procurement | GeM / CPP / open tender | Competitive bidding with concession period (15–30 yrs) |
| Operation | Government department | Private concessionaire for fixed term, then transfer |
| Example | Kochi Bypass (PWD) | **Kochi Metro Rail** (GoI + GoK + French Agency) |

*(3 marks: comparison + examples)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 carries **one full 14-mark question with internal choice (a) or (b)**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Classification & Stakeholder Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with Dec 2023 pattern]**

> **(a)** Classify construction projects in **four different ways**, giving **two examples** for each class. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Identify and explain the **role, primary interest, and engagement strategy** of **any six stakeholders** in a construction project. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

The four classifications are:

**1. By Nature of Work**
- *Building* — Residential flats, office complex.
- *Infrastructure* — NH-66 widening, Kochi Metro viaduct.
- *Industrial* — Refinery expansion, cement plant.
- *Specialty* — Piling, HVAC retrofitting.

**2. By Sector**
- *Public* — PWD school building.
- *Private* — Private hospital.
- *PPP* — Smart City Kochi.

**3. By Delivery Method**
- *Design-Bid-Build* — Traditional CPWD contract.
- *Design-Build / EPC* — Single contractor for design + build.
- *BOT/BOOT* — Toll road with 20-year concession.
- *Turnkey* — Owner hands over land; gets a fully operational facility.

**4. By Size**
- Small (< ₹ 1 Cr), Medium (₹ 1–50 Cr), Large (₹ 50–500 Cr), Mega (> ₹ 500 Cr).

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per classification + 0.5 per example ≈ full marks)*

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

| # | Stakeholder | Role | Primary Interest | Engagement Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Owner** | Funds and decides | ROI, functionality | **Manage Closely** (high power + high interest) |
| 2 | **PMC Consultant** | Owner's agent | Schedule, cost, quality | Manage Closely |
| 3 | **Design Consultant** | Design conversion | Design integrity, codes | Keep Informed |
| 4 | **Contractor** | Builds | Profit, safety, payment | Manage Closely |
| 5 | **Sub-contractor** | Specialised work | Scope clarity, payment | Keep Informed |
| 6 | **Local Community** | Affected by works | Pollution, traffic | Keep Informed |
| 7 | **Regulator** (Municipality) | Approvals | Code compliance | Keep Satisfied |
| 8 | **Bank / Financier** | Loan | Repayment | Manage Closely |
| 9 | **End User** | Uses the facility | Quality, usability | Keep Informed |
| 10 | **Insurance** | Risk transfer | Premium, claim validity | Monitor |

*(7 marks: 6 stakeholders × ~1.2 marks each)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Life Cycle & Success Criteria*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with June 2024 pattern]**

> **(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **four phases of the Construction Project Life Cycle (CPLC)**. For each phase, mention the **key deliverable** and **dominant stakeholder**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Iron Triangle** of project success and discuss how modern construction project management has **expanded** this concept. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

| Phase | Key Activity | Key Deliverable | Dominant Stakeholder | % of Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Conceptual / Feasibility** | Feasibility study, DPR, land identification | Feasibility Report + Go/No-Go | Owner | 1–3% |
| **2. Planning & Design** | Site investigation, detailed design, statutory approvals | Approved drawings + BOQ + Tender | Consultant | 5–10% |
| **3. Execution** | Mobilisation, substructure, superstructure, services, finishing | Physical built facility | Contractor | **80–90%** |
| **4. Closure & Handover** | Testing, snagging, DLP, as-builts | Handover certificate, closure report | Owner + PMC | 2–5% |

**Sketch:** (Refer to Mermaid CPLC diagram in Section 4.2 — reproduce as labelled box-arrow flow on answer script.)

**Critical Insight:** Phase 3 (Execution) consumes the **bulk of cost and carries the highest risk**, which is why *scheduling, monitoring, and resource management modules* (Modules 2, 3, 4) receive maximum weightage in PBCET604.

*(7 marks: ~1.5 per phase + 0.5 for sketch/insight)*

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**The Iron Triangle (Traditional View):**

The three classic constraints of any project are:

$$\text{Scope} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Time} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Cost}$$

with **Quality** at the centre (affected by all three). A change in any one constraint inevitably affects the other two — for example, *compressing time* typically *increases cost* and may *reduce scope* or *compromise quality*.

**Modern Expansion (KTU 2024 View):**

Contemporary construction project management recognises that success is **multidimensional**:

$$\text{Modern Success} = \text{Iron Triangle} + \text{Safety} + \text{Environment} + \text{Stakeholder Satisfaction} + \text{Sustainability}$$

- **Safety:** Zero-fatality target; mandatory under BOCW Act & Factories Act.
- **Environment:** Compliance with EIA Notification 2006, CPCB/SPCB norms, IGBC ratings.
- **Stakeholder Satisfaction:** Beyond owner — includes community, users, regulators.
- **Sustainability (NEP 2020 emphasis):** Net-zero, GRIHA/IGBC rating, life-cycle carbon.

**Conclusion:** A project delivered *on time, within budget, and to scope* but with *a worker fatality, a polluted river, and an unhappy community* is no longer judged as a *successful* project in the KTU 2024 framework.

*(7 marks: 3 marks triangle + 4 marks modern expansion with examples)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 1):**
> 1. **Skipping definitions** — Always start with a one-line definition. Examiners allocate **1 mark purely for the opening definition**.
> 2. **Listing without explaining** — "*Owner, Contractor, Consultant*" alone is worth **0 marks**. Add role, interest, and engagement strategy to score.
> 3. **Conflating "project" and "operations"** — Maintenance of a hospital is **not** a project; construction of a new hospital block **is**.
> 4. **Forgetting the modern pillars** — Mentioning only "Iron Triangle" in 2024 scheme is **outdated**; always add Safety, Environment, Stakeholder Satisfaction.
> 5. **Missing examples** — Kerala-specific examples (Kochi Metro, Smart City, GIFT-style townships) fetch extra impression points.
> 6. **No sketch in life-cycle question** — A 14-mark CPLC question without a *labelled phase-flow diagram* typically loses **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card — Print & Carry**

- **Definition:** Temporary + Unique + Goal-oriented → built facility.
- **5 Characteristics:** Temporary, Unique, Progressive Elaboration, Resource-Constrained, Stakeholder-Driven.
- **4 Classifications:** By Nature (Building/Infrastructure/Industrial/Specialty), By Sector (Public/Private/PPP), By Delivery (DBB/DB-EPC/Turnkey/BOT-BOOT), By Size (Small/Medium/Large/Mega).
- **4 Phases of CPLC:** Concept (2%) → Planning (8%) → **Execution (85%)** → Closure (5%).
- **Execution Phase = 80–90% of cost = Most Critical Phase.**
- **Minimum 8–10 Stakeholders** expected in any answer: Owner, PMC, Designer, Contractor, Sub-contractor, Supplier, Regulator, User, Financier, Community.
- **Mendelow Matrix** = Power × Interest → Manage Closely / Keep Satisfied / Keep Informed / Monitor.
- **Iron Triangle:** Scope ↔ Time ↔ Cost; Quality at centre.
- **Modern Pillars:** + Safety + Environment + Stakeholder Satisfaction + **Sustainability (NEP 2020)**.
- **Cost Estimation Accuracy** in construction is **± 10–30%** — much lower than manufacturing (± 2–5%).
- **Kerala-specific examples to remember:** Kochi Metro, Smart City Kochi, CIAL, GIFT City-style proposals, KIIFB-funded projects.
- **Statutory Acts to recall:** RERA 2016, BOCW Act 1996, Factories Act 1948, EIA Notification 2006, NBC 2016, KMBR (Kerala Municipal Building Rules).
- **Most-likely 14-mark question format:** *Classify + Stakeholders* **OR** *Life Cycle + Success Criteria*. Practice both.

---

**End of Module 1 — Topic: Construction Projects**

*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PBCET604 | Course Outcome CO1 | Bloom Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply*

topics.title: Life Cycle of a Project - Phases in a Project. Tendering: Types of Tenders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET604 - CONSTRUCTION PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Life Cycle of a Project - Phases in a Project. Tendering: Types of Tenders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Project Life Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The **Project Life Cycle (PLC)** refers to the series of phases that a project passes through from its initiation to its closure. It provides a framework for managing the project effectively, defining the beginning, the operational middle, and the end of the project.

In the context of **Construction Project Management**, the project life cycle represents the sequential, often overlapping stages a construction project undergoes — from the moment a *need* is identified (e.g., a new flyover in Kochi) to the moment the *facility is handed over* and the contract is formally closed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a House"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the project life cycle as the journey of *building your own home*:
> - **Conception (Dream Phase):** "I need a new house." — you realize the need.
> - **Planning (Blueprint Phase):** You consult an architect, draw plans, estimate cost, arrange a bank loan.
> - **Execution (Construction Phase):** Masons, electricians, and plumbers work daily; you monitor progress.
> - **Closure (Handover Phase):** You take possession of the keys, the contractor submits the final bill, and the project is formally complete.
> 
> Just like in a house, *you cannot plaster the walls before laying the foundation*. Each phase has a logical order.

### 1.3 Definition of Tendering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** **Tendering** is the process of preparing and submitting a competitive offer (tender) by a contractor to carry out specified work, or by an owner/client inviting such offers. It is the primary mechanism for **procurement of construction services** in KTU-aligned public and private projects.

### 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU Exams

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Owner / Client** | The organization commissioning the project (e.g., PWD, KSRTC, NHAI). |
| **Consultant** | The architect/engineer who designs and supervises. |
| **Contractor** | The firm that executes the construction work. |
| **Tender Notice** | A public advertisement inviting bids. |
| **Bid / Offer** | A formal priced proposal submitted by a contractor. |
| **Award** | Formal acceptance of a bid by the owner, leading to a contract. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always distinguish between *Tendering* (the *invitation & selection* process) and *Contract Management* (the *execution & administration* of the awarded contract). They are different modules in PBCET604.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of a Construction Project Life Cycle

The project life cycle is universally structured into **5 sequential phases** (per PMBOK & KTU syllabus):

#### Phase 1: Project Initiation / Conception
- The **need** is identified (e.g., traffic congestion → need for a bypass).
- A **Feasibility Study** is conducted (technical, economic, legal, operational, scheduling — **TELOS**).
- A **Project Charter** is prepared, formally authorizing the project.
- Key output: **Project Initiation Document (PID)**.

#### Phase 2: Project Planning / Definition
- The most **intellectually intensive** phase.
- **Detailed Engineering** (architectural, structural, MEP designs).
- **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is created.
- **Cost estimation**, **Scheduling** (Gantt charts, CPM, PERT), and **Resource planning**.
- **Tendering documents** (drawings, Bill of Quantities, technical specifications) are prepared.
- Key output: **Project Management Plan (PMP)**.

#### Phase 3: Project Execution / Implementation
- The **most resource-intensive** phase (typically 70–80% of project budget spent here).
- **Site mobilization**, procurement of materials, hiring of labor.
- **Construction** as per drawings and specifications.
- **Quality control**, **safety management**, and **subcontractor coordination**.
- **Progress reports** submitted periodically.
- Key output: **Deliverables (the actual built structure)**.

#### Phase 4: Project Monitoring & Controlling
- Runs **parallel to execution** (not a separate time block).
- Tracks the *triple constraint*: **Scope, Time, Cost**.
- **Earned Value Management (EVM)**, variance analysis, schedule updates.
- **Change management** (handling design changes, extra items, deviation statements).
- Corrective and preventive actions are taken to keep the project on track.

#### Phase 5: Project Closure / Completion
- **Final inspection** and **handover** of the facility.
- **Testing & commissioning** of systems (e.g., elevators, electrical panels).
- **As-built drawings** submitted by the contractor.
- **Final payment**, **performance security release**, and **contract closure**.
- **Post-project review / lessons learned** documented for future projects.
- Key output: **Project Closure Report**.

### 2.2 Standard Project Effort Distribution (KTU High-Yield Data)

| Phase | Typical % of Total Project Duration | Typical % of Project Effort/Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Initiation | 5% – 10% | 5% |
| Planning | 15% – 25% | 15% |
| Execution | 50% – 60% | 70% – 80% |
| Monitoring & Control | Continuous | 5% – 10% |
| Closure | 5% – 10% | 5% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common KTU 14-mark question is *"Explain the phases of a project life cycle with the relative effort distribution."* Use the data above in your answer.

### 2.3 Key Characteristics of a Project Life Cycle

- **Cost & Staffing are LOW at the start**, PEAK during execution, and DROP sharply at closure.
- **Risk & Uncertainty are HIGHEST at initiation** and **LOWEST at closure** (as more is known).
- **Influence of stakeholders is HIGHEST at the start** and decreases over time.
- Successive phases tend to have a **shorter duration but higher cost intensity** (the classic *S-curve* of cumulative cost vs time).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Project Life Cycle Concepts

| Concept | Description / Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **BCR** (Benefit-Cost Ratio) | $BCR = \frac{\text{Present Value of Benefits}}{\text{Present Value of Costs}}$ | Dimensionless |
| **NPV** (Net Present Value) | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Currency (₹) |
| **Schedule Variance (SV)** | $SV = EV - PV$ | Days / Currency |
| **Cost Variance (CV)** | $CV = EV - AC$ | Currency (₹) |
| **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** | $CPI = \frac{EV}{AC}$ | Ratio |
| **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** | $SPI = \frac{EV}{PV}$ | Ratio |

> [!TIP]
> Where **EV** = Earned Value, **PV** = Planned Value, **AC** = Actual Cost, $r$ = discount rate, $B_t$ = benefit at time $t$, $C_t$ = cost at time $t$.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Understanding the project life cycle helps construction managers:
- **Allocate budgets** accurately to each phase.
- **Identify risk hotspots** (concept and execution phases).
- **Coordinate stakeholders** (client, consultant, contractor, statutory bodies).
- **Plan cash flow** and avoid mid-project financial shocks.

---

### 2.6 Tendering — Process and Types

#### 2.6.1 The Tendering Process Flow

1. **Need identification** → Decision to construct.
2. **Preparation of Tender Documents**: drawings, BOQ, specifications, contract conditions.
3. **Notification**: Publication of tender notice (newspaper, e-tender portal like Kerala Govt. e-tender).
4. **Sale of Tender Documents**: to interested bidders for a nominal fee.
5. **Pre-bid Meeting**: clarification of doubts by the client/consultant.
6. **Bid Submission**: contractors submit their priced bids by the deadline.
7. **Bid Opening**: conducted publicly; recorded.
8. **Bid Evaluation**: technical and financial scrutiny.
9. **Award of Contract**: Letter of Acceptance (LOA) issued to the lowest/qualified bidder.
10. **Agreement Signing & Work Order**: formal contract signed; work begins.

#### 2.6.2 Types of Tenders (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Type of Tender | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Open Tender** | Publicly advertised; any qualified contractor can bid. Most transparent. | Government / Public works (PWD, KSRTC, NHAI). |
| 2 | **Selective / Restricted Tender** | Limited contractors (pre-qualified) are invited. | Specialized works (e.g., pile foundations, HVAC). |
| 3 | **Negotiated Tender** | Single contractor is approached directly and terms negotiated. | Emergency, proprietary, or highly specialized work. |
| 4 | **Serial Tender** | A contractor from a pre-existing list is engaged for similar work. | Maintenance, minor repairs. |
| 5 | **Single Tender / Nomination** | A specific contractor is "nominated" without competition. | Highly confidential or specialized work. |
| 6 | **Package Tender** | Whole project (design + build) is tendered as one. | Turnkey / EPC contracts. |
| 7 | **Turnkey Tender** | Contractor designs, builds, and hands over the ready facility. | Industrial projects, airports. |
| 8 | **E-Tender (Electronic Tender)** | Conducted online through government portals. | Mandatory for all Central/State govt. works in India. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Open Tendering is the **most commonly asked** type. Always mention that it follows the **L1 (Lowest bidder) system** in Indian government works.

#### 2.6.3 Advantages and Disadvantages (Key Comparison)

| Type | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Open Tender** | Maximum competition, transparency, fair price. | Time-consuming; risk of unqualified bidders. |
| **Selective Tender** | Quality assured; faster evaluation. | Limited competition; possible favouritism. |
| **Negotiated Tender** | Fast; flexible; suitable for complex or urgent jobs. | No competition; risk of inflated cost. |
| **E-Tender** | Transparent, fast, paperless, wider reach. | Requires digital literacy; server issues. |

#### 2.6.4 Key Tender Documents (Required for KTU Answers)

1. **Notice Inviting Tender (NIT)**
2. **General Conditions of Contract (GCC)**
3. **Special / Particular Conditions of Contract**
4. **Drawings (Tender Drawings)**
5. **Bill of Quantities (BOQ) / Schedule of Quantities**
6. **Technical Specifications**
7. **Form of Agreement**
8. **Earnest Money Deposit (EMD)** — typically **2% of estimated cost**.
9. **Performance Security** — typically **5% of contract value**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard EMD:** For Kerala PWD works, EMD is generally **1–2% of the estimated cost** (refundable); Performance Security is **5% of contract value** (refundable after defect liability period).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Net Present Value (NPV) for Project Feasibility

The **NPV** is a foundational KTU concept used during the **Initiation Phase** to decide whether to proceed with a construction project. Below is the step-by-step derivation.

**Step 1 — State the principle.**
A rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received tomorrow, because today's rupee can earn interest. We must therefore "discount" future cash flows to a *present equivalent* before adding them.

**Step 2 — Define discount factor for year $t$.**
If $r$ is the annual discount rate, then a sum of ₹1 received after $t$ years is equivalent today to:

$$
DF_t = \frac{1}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 3 — Define present value of a single future cash flow.**
If $CF_t$ is the cash flow (benefit minus cost) in year $t$, its present value is:

$$
PV_t = CF_t \cdot DF_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum over the project life $n$.**
The total Net Present Value of the project is the sum of all discounted cash flows:

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision rule.**
If $NPV > 0$ → project is **financially viable** (accept).
If $NPV < 0$ → project is **not viable** (reject).
If $NPV = 0$ → indifference point.

**Step 6 — Worked Example (KTU style).**
A small bridge project has the following expected net cash flows at a discount rate of $r = 10\%$. Determine NPV.

| Year ($t$) | Net Cash Flow $CF_t$ (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| 0 | $-100$ (initial investment) |
| 1 | $+30$ |
| 2 | $+40$ |
| 3 | $+50$ |
| 4 | $+30$ |

**Step 6a — Compute each discounted cash flow.**

$$
PV_0 = \frac{-100}{(1.10)^0} = -100.00
$$

$$
PV_1 = \frac{+30}{(1.10)^1} = \frac{30}{1.10} = +27.27
$$

$$
PV_2 = \frac{+40}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{40}{1.21} = +33.06
$$

$$
PV_3 = \frac{+50}{(1.10)^3} = \frac{50}{1.331} = +37.57
$$

$$
PV_4 = \frac{+30}{(1.10)^4} = \frac{30}{1.4641} = +20.49
$$

**Step 6b — Sum the present values.**

$$
NPV = -100.00 + 27.27 + 33.06 + 37.57 + 20.49
$$

$$
NPV = +18.39 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 6c — Interpretation.**
Since $NPV = +18.39 > 0$, the bridge project is **financially viable** and should be recommended during the Initiation Phase.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Tender Type

Below is a decision matrix that mirrors what a KTU examiner expects in a *selecting the right type of tender* question.

| Project Scenario | Recommended Tender Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Government PWD road project in Kerala | **Open Tender** | Transparency, public accountability, L1 system. |
| Repair of a leaking roof in a 50-year-old KSRTC depot | **Serial / Maintenance Tender** | Repetitive, low value, trusted contractors. |
| Confidential defence infrastructure | **Negotiated / Nomination** | Security, classified specifications. |
| Design + Build of a 5-star hotel | **Turnkey / Package Tender** | Single point of responsibility, faster delivery. |
| Specialized retrofitting (e.g., base isolation for heritage building) | **Selective Tender** | Only a few experts can do it; quality matters. |
| Large EPC solar park | **E-Tender (Global Open)** | Wide reach, transparent, standard EPC contracts. |

### 3.3 Key Numerical Relations Used in Tendering

**Formula 1: Earnest Money Deposit (EMD) amount.**

$$
EMD = \text{EMD Percentage} \times \text{Estimated Project Cost}
$$

**Formula 2: Performance Security amount.**

$$
PS = \text{PS Percentage} \times \text{Contract Value}
$$

**Formula 3: Security Deposit (often deducted from running bills).**

$$
SD = \text{SD Percentage} \times \text{Each Running Bill}
$$

**Formula 4: Retention Money (kept from each bill, released after DLP).**

$$
RM = \text{RM Percentage} \times \text{Each Running Bill}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **DLP** = **Defect Liability Period** (typically **6 to 24 months** for KTU/PWD works; 24 months for building works, 6 months for road works).

**Worked Example (Tender Financials):**
For a Kerala PWD building project with:
- Estimated cost = ₹ **2 Crore**
- EMD = **2%**
- Performance Security = **5%**
- L1 Bid (lowest) = ₹ **1.85 Crore**

**Step A — EMD to be deposited with bid:**

$$
EMD = 0.02 \times 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step B — Performance Security (based on contract value, not estimated cost):**

$$
PS = 0.05 \times 1{,}85{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 9{,}25{,}000
$$

**Step C — Net Contract Value to be paid over project life:**

$$
\text{Net Payable} = 1.85\ \text{Cr} - 9.25\ \text{Lakhs (held as PS)}
$$

This kind of calculation is often asked as a *3-mark short problem* in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Project Life Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Project Need Identified] --> phase1A[Phase 1 Initiation and Conception]
    phase1A --> doc1A[Feasibility Study and Project Charter]
    doc1A --> phase2A[Phase 2 Project Planning and Definition]
    phase2A --> doc2A[Detailed Design WBS BOQ CPM Schedule]
    doc2A --> phase3A[Phase 3 Project Execution and Implementation]
    phase3A --> doc3A[Site Mobilization Construction QC and Safety]
    doc3A --> phase4A[Phase 4 Project Monitoring and Controlling]
    phase4A --> doc4A[Earned Value Analysis Change Control Reports]
    doc4A --> phase5A[Phase 5 Project Closure and Handover]
    doc5A[Final Inspection As Built Drawings Final Payment]
    phase5A --> doc5A
    doc5A --> endA[Project Closed and Lessons Documented]
```

### 4.2 Effort and Risk Curves Across the Life Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisA[Time Axis] --> p1A[Initiation]
    p1A --> p2A[Planning]
    p2A --> p3A[Execution]
    p3A --> p4A[Monitoring and Control]
    p4A --> p5A[Closure]
    riskCurveA[Risk and Uncertainty HIGH then LOW] -.-> p1A
    riskCurveA -.-> p5A
    effortCurveA[Cost and Staffing LOW then PEAK then DROP] -.-> p1A
    effortCurveA -.-> p5A
```

### 4.3 Tendering Process Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[Project Defined] --> n1A[Prepare Tender Documents]
    n1A --> n2A[Publish NIT Notice]
    n2A --> n3A[Sale of Tender Forms]
    n3A --> n4A[Pre Bid Meeting]
    n4A --> n5A[Submission of Bids with EMD]
    n5A --> n6A[Public Bid Opening]
    n6A --> n7A[Bid Evaluation Technical and Financial]
    n7A --> n8A[Letter of Acceptance LOA]
    n8A --> n9A[Performance Security Deposit]
    n9A --> n10A[Signing of Agreement and Work Order]
    n10A --> endB[Construction Phase Begins]
```

### 4.4 Tender Classification — Hierarchical Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    tenderRootA[Tendering System] --> openA[Open Tender L1 Public]
    tenderRootA --> selA[Selective Tender Pre Qualified]
    tenderRootA --> negA[Negotiated Tender Direct]
    tenderRootA --> serA[Serial Tender Maintenance]
    tenderRootA --> nomA[Nomination Single Party]
    tenderRootA --> pkgA[Package or Turnkey Tender]
    tenderRootA --> etdA[E Tender Online Portal]
    openA --> govA[Most used in Kerala PWD NHAI KSRTC]
    selA --> specA[Specialized Quality Critical Works]
    negA --> emergA[Emergency Security Classified Works]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]** Define the term "Project Life Cycle" and list its various phases.

**Model Answer:**
The Project Life Cycle is the series of phases that a project passes through from its initiation to its closure. The phases are:
1. **Initiation / Conception** – need identification, feasibility study.
2. **Planning / Definition** – design, estimation, scheduling, tender docs.
3. **Execution / Implementation** – actual construction.
4. **Monitoring & Controlling** – running parallel to execution.
5. **Closure / Handover** – final inspection, payment, documentation.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for phases list)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]** Differentiate between Open Tender and Negotiated Tender.

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Open Tender | Negotiated Tender |
|---|---|---|
| **Invitation** | Publicly advertised | Direct approach to one contractor |
| **Competition** | Maximum | Nil / minimal |
| **Transparency** | High | Low |
| **Speed** | Slow | Fast |
| **Use** | Government / public works | Emergency / proprietary / confidential works |

*(3 marks — table-based comparison)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing generic "phases" without naming *typical outputs* (Charter, PMP, Deliverables, Closure Report) — examiners deduct 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *Earnest Money Deposit (EMD)* with *Security Deposit (SD)*. They are different: EMD is bid-stage; SD is contract-stage.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not drawing any diagram for life cycle. **Always draw a block diagram** — it alone fetches 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "Negotiated Tender = corruption". It is a *legitimate procurement method* in specific scenarios (emergency, proprietary). Use professional language.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Compulsory for 2024 Scheme, Module 1

**[KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Model, PBCET604]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various phases of a Project Life Cycle. Sketch a neat block diagram showing the relative level of effort and risk across each phase. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A construction project life cycle consists of **five phases**:

1. **Initiation Phase:**
   - Identification of need (e.g., new bridge, hospital).
   - Conducted: Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Scheduling feasibility (TELOS).
   - Output: *Project Charter*; stakeholders identified; budget sanctioned in principle.

2. **Planning Phase:**
   - Detailed engineering (architectural, structural, MEP).
   - Preparation of Bill of Quantities, drawings, specifications.
   - Work Breakdown Structure, Gantt Chart, CPM/PERT schedule.
   - Risk register and procurement plan.
   - Output: *Project Management Plan (PMP)* and *Tender Documents*.

3. **Execution Phase:**
   - Site mobilization, material procurement, labour deployment.
   - Construction as per drawings; quality control tests (cube tests, slump tests, soil tests).
   - Coordination among subcontractors, statutory inspections.
   - Output: *Physical Deliverables* (the constructed facility) and *Progress Reports*.

4. **Monitoring & Controlling Phase:**
   - Runs parallel to execution.
   - Earned Value Analysis, Schedule Variance, Cost Variance.
   - Change management: handling extra items, substitutions, deviation orders.
   - Output: *Corrective Action Plans* and *Updated Schedule*.

5. **Closure Phase:**
   - Final inspection, testing & commissioning.
   - Submission of as-built drawings, O&M manuals.
   - Final bill settlement, release of Performance Security.
   - Project Closure Report and *Lessons Learned* documentation.

**Diagram (Block flow — see Section 4.1 of these notes).**

*Relative effort and risk:*
- *Effort & cost:* Low → High (peak during execution) → Low at closure.
- *Risk & uncertainty:* High at initiation → Lowest at closure.

*(7 marks — distribute as 1 mark per phase description + 2 marks for the diagram & effort/risk commentary)*

---

**(b)** What is tendering? Explain the different types of tenders with suitable examples from the construction industry. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Tendering is the systematic process of inviting, receiving, and evaluating competitive offers (bids) for executing construction work, leading to the award of a contract.

**Types of Tenders:**

1. **Open Tender:**
   Publicly advertised; any qualified contractor can bid.
   *Example:* Kerala PWD tender for a state highway repair.
   *System:* Lowest bidder (L1) wins.

2. **Selective (Restricted) Tender:**
   Pre-qualified contractors on a panel are invited.
   *Example:* Specialist pile foundation work in marine clay.

3. **Negotiated Tender:**
   Direct negotiation with a single contractor.
   *Example:* Emergency repair after a bridge collapse.

4. **Serial Tender:**
   Rotating list of contractors for repetitive work.
   *Example:* Annual maintenance contract of a campus.

5. **Nomination Tender:**
   A specific contractor is chosen without competition.
   *Example:* Highly confidential defence installation.

6. **Package / Turnkey Tender:**
   Single contractor handles design + build + handover.
   *Example:* EPC contract for a solar park.

7. **E-Tender:**
   Conducted online via government portals.
   *Example:* All Central Public Works Department (CPWD) tenders in India.

**Key Tender Documents:** NIT, GCC, BOQ, Drawings, EMD (≈2%), Performance Security (≈5%).

*(7 marks — 1 mark for definition + 5 marks for types with examples + 1 mark for documents)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Model, PBCET604]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of feasibility study in the Initiation Phase of a construction project. What are the various aspects covered under it? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Feasibility Study** is conducted during the **Initiation Phase** to answer the question: *"Should this project be undertaken at all?"* It reduces risk and provides a scientific basis for investment decisions.

**Aspects Covered (TELOS):**

1. **Technical Feasibility:** Are the required technology, materials, and expertise available? Soil conditions, climate, design constraints.
2. **Economic / Financial Feasibility:** NPV, BCR, IRR, Payback Period. Affordability of the client and funding sources.
3. **Legal Feasibility:** Land ownership, statutory clearances (Kerala Panchayat/ Municipality, CRZ, Environmental Clearance, KSPCB NOC).
4. **Operational Feasibility:** Will the end user be able to operate and maintain the facility post-construction?
5. **Scheduling Feasibility:** Can the project be completed within the desired timeframe?

**Output of Initiation Phase:** Project Charter, sanctioned budget, appointed PMC, and decision to proceed to Planning Phase.

*(7 marks — 1 mark for role + 5 marks for the 5 aspects with examples + 1 mark for outputs)*

---

**(b)** Compare Open Tender, Selective Tender, and Negotiated Tender in a tabular form. Under what circumstances would you recommend a Negotiated Tender despite its disadvantages? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Open Tender | Selective Tender | Negotiated Tender |
|---|---|---|---|
| Competition | Maximum | Limited | None |
| Transparency | Very high | Moderate | Low |
| Time required | Long | Medium | Very short |
| Cost outcome | Lowest (often) | Reasonable | May be high |
| Suitability | Public/government works | Specialized works | Emergency / classified |
| Risk of favouritism | Low | Medium | High |

**Circumstances justifying Negotiated Tender:**

1. **Emergency situations** (post-disaster reconstruction, sudden structural failure).
2. **Proprietary / patented systems** (e.g., specialized bearings, specific software-controlled systems).
3. **National security or classified** projects.
4. **Continuity** — when an existing contractor is already familiar with the site and re-tendering is impractical.
5. **Negotiated procurement** is permitted under the **General Financial Rules (GFR), 2017** of Govt. of India in such exceptional cases.

*(7 marks — 4 marks for table + 3 marks for justified circumstances)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Project Life Cycle** = Initiation → Planning → Execution → Monitoring → Closure.
- **Cost and staffing** are *lowest at start, peak during execution, drop at closure*.
- **Risk and influence** are *highest at start, lowest at closure*.
- The **Planning Phase** produces the *Tender Documents*.
- **Tendering** is a *separate process* from contract management; it ends with **Letter of Acceptance (LOA)**.
- **Open Tender** = public + L1 system; widely used in Kerala PWD works.
- **Selective Tender** = pre-qualified contractors; used for specialized jobs.
- **Negotiated Tender** = direct negotiation; used for emergencies / proprietary work.
- **Serial Tender** = used for routine maintenance.
- **Turnkey / Package Tender** = design + build + handover by one contractor.
- **E-Tender** = mandatory for all central/state govt. works in India.
- **EMD** ≈ **2% of estimated cost**; refundable.
- **Performance Security** ≈ **5% of contract value**; refundable after Defect Liability Period (DLP).
- **DLP** = 6 to 24 months depending on the nature of the work.
- **NPV > 0** → project is financially viable.
- **Always draw a block diagram** for life cycle / tendering flow questions — easy 2 marks.
- Remember: **PMBOK, FIDIC, CPWD, and Kerala PWD Manual** are the standard reference frameworks used in KTU question papers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Hack:** If the question is *"Explain the phases of a project life cycle"*, write each phase as a sub-heading with **Inputs → Activities → Outputs**. This structured format is exactly what KTU examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stages in Tendering. Process of Development of Plans and Schedules - Work Break-down Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET604 - CONSTRUCTION PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stages in Tendering. Process of Development of Plans and Schedules - Work Break-down Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tendering — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tendering** is the formal, structured, and transparent procurement process by which an owner/client invites qualified contractors to submit competitive sealed offers (bids/tenders) for the execution of a specific construction project, based on detailed tender documents that define the scope, specifications, conditions of contract, and commercial terms.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PBCET604 – Construction Project Management**, tendering is treated as a *project procurement function* that bridges the **planning phase** and the **execution phase** of a construction project. It is governed by principles of fairness, transparency, accountability, and value-for-money (V3M: Value, Viability, Money).

## 1.2 Stages in Tendering — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stages in Tendering** refer to the sequential, chronological phases of the procurement cycle, beginning from the *identification of project need* and culminating in the *award of contract* to the successful bidder. Each stage has legally and procedurally defined deliverables.

The standard KTU-recommended taxonomy identifies **six (6) core stages**:

| Stage # | Stage Name | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Project Need Identification & Feasibility | Need Statement, DPR Outline |
| 2 | Tender Document Preparation | Tender Notice, BOQ, Drawings, GCC/SCC |
| 3 | Tender Publication & Sale | Published NIT / e-Tender |
| 4 | Bid Submission & Pre-Bid Process | Sealed Bids, Clarifications |
| 5 | Bid Evaluation & Comparative Statement | Evaluation Report |
| 6 | Award of Contract & Agreement Signing | Letter of Award (LOA), Contract Agreement |

## 1.3 Work Break-down Structure (WBS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Break-down Structure (WBS)** is a hierarchical, deliverable-oriented decomposition of the total scope of work of a construction project into progressively smaller, more manageable, and individually estimable work packages. It is the foundational element of project planning, scheduling, costing, and control, as defined by the *Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK)* and adopted in the KTU 2024 CPM syllabus.

Mathematically expressed as a recursive partitioning:

$$W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} W_{ij} = \dots = \sum W_{leaf}$$

where each leaf node represents the **lowest-level Work Package (WP)** that can be *estimated, scheduled, monitored, and controlled*.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Tendering)**: Imagine you want to host a *wedding banquet* for 500 guests. You cannot just call one caterer and accept the first quote. Instead, you would:
> 1. List your needs (menu, venue, decoration).
> 2. Prepare a *Request for Quote (RFQ)* with all specifications.
> 3. Invite 5–6 caterers to submit their best offers.
> 4. Compare prices, reviews, and menus.
> 5. Award the contract to the best caterer and sign a written agreement.
>
> This is **tendering** — competitive, transparent, documented procurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (WBS)**: Suppose you are planning a *road-trip from Kasaragod to Thiruvananthapuram*. The total trip is broken into:
> - Leg 1: Kasaragod → Kozhikode
> - Leg 2: Kozhikode → Kochi
> - Leg 3: Kochi → TVM
>
> Each leg is further split into *driving hours, fuel stops, meals, rest*. This **top-down decomposition** is exactly how a WBS works for a construction project.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Stages of Tendering — Analytical Breakdown

### Stage 1: Project Need Identification & Feasibility
- **Why:** No tender can be floated without a *justified project need* backed by a feasibility study.
- **How:** Owner identifies requirement → Preliminary survey → Demand assessment → Feasibility Report (Technical + Financial + Legal + Operational — the *4 pillars*).
- **Key Output:** *Detailed Project Report (DPR)*, *Administrative Sanction*, *Techno-Economic Feasibility (TEFR)*.
- **KTU Emphasis:** Students must remember that **public-sector tenders in Kerala** (PWD, KWA, KSRDC) require *Administrative Sanction* and *Technical Sanction* before any NIT (Notice Inviting Tender) is published.

### Stage 2: Tender Document Preparation
- **Why:** Bidders must have identical, unambiguous information to bid fairly.
- **How:** Preparation of the *Tender Document Set* comprising:
  1. **Notice Inviting Tender (NIT)** — public invitation.
  2. **Instructions to Bidders (ITB)** — procedural rules.
  3. **General Conditions of Contract (GCC)** — boilerplate legal terms.
  4. **Special Conditions of Contract (SCC)** — project-specific overrides.
  5. **Technical Specifications** — quality criteria.
  6. **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** — quantity takeoff.
  7. **Drawings** — graphical scope.
  8. **Form of Contract / Agreement** — legal instrument.
- **KTU Emphasis:** **BOQ** is the *pricing spine* of the tender; errors in BOQ quantification are the most common source of disputes.

### Stage 3: Tender Publication & Sale
- **Why:** Transparency mandates *public* dissemination.
- **How:** Publication in newspapers, government gazettes, official websites, and **e-procurement portals** (Kerala: *e-Procurement Kerala* / *etenders.kerala.gov.in*).
- **Cost of Tender Document:** Typically **0.1% to 0.5% of estimated project cost**, non-refundable.
- **Earnest Money Deposit (EMD):** Typically **1% to 2% of estimated cost**, refundable.

### Stage 4: Bid Submission & Pre-Bid Process
- **Why:** Bidders need clarifications; owner needs sealed offers.
- **How:**
  - **Pre-Bid Meeting:** Held 7–14 days before submission deadline. Minutes are issued as *corrigendum*.
  - **Bid Submission:** Two-envelope system (Technical + Financial) OR e-bid (encrypted upload).
  - **Bid Security / EMD:** Submitted along with bid.
- **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse EMD with Performance Security — they are different instruments.

### Stage 5: Bid Evaluation & Comparative Statement
- **Why:** Owner must select *most suitable* bid, not just lowest.
- **How:** Multi-criteria evaluation:
  - **L1 (Lowest Evaluated Cost)** — for item-rate/percentage tenders.
  - **QCBS (Quality & Cost Based Selection)** — for consultancy/EPC tenders.
  - **Least-Cost Selection (LCS)** — for standardized works.
- **Comparative Statement:** Tabulates all bids; flags deviations, arithmetic errors, and non-responsive bids.
- **KTU Emphasis:** *Arithmetical errors in Bids* are corrected by the tendering authority as per standard procedures (CVC / CPWD guidelines).

### Stage 6: Award of Contract & Agreement Signing
- **Why:** Legally binding execution.
- **How:**
  1. **Letter of Acceptance (LOA)** issued to L1 bidder.
  2. **Performance Security** (typically **5%–10% of contract value**) deposited by contractor within 28 days.
  3. **Contract Agreement** signed within stipulated period (usually 14–28 days).
  4. **Work Order / Notice to Proceed (NTP)** issued.
  5. **Mobilization Advance** (up to **10%**) released if applicable.

## 2.2 Work Break-down Structure — Theoretical Analysis

### The 100% Rule
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **100% Rule** of WBS states that the WBS must represent **100% of the project scope** — no more, no less. Every deliverable, including project management itself, must be captured.

### Decomposition Levels (Standard KTU Convention)
| Level | Nomenclature | Example (Highway Project) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Project | 4-laning of NH-66 (Kochi Section) |
| 1 | Major Deliverables | Earthwork, Pavement, Bridges |
| 2 | Sub-Deliverables | Embankment, Subgrade, GSB, WMM |
| 3 | Work Packages | Earthwork in Cutting, Earthwork in Filling |
| 4 | Activities | Excavation in hard rock, Loosening, Loading |
| 5 | Tasks | Operator deployment, Diesel consumption |

### WBS vs. OBS vs. CBS
- **WBS (Work):** Decomposes *deliverables*.
- **OBS (Organizational Break-down Structure):** Decomposes *the organization / reporting hierarchy*.
- **CBS (Cost Break-down Structure):** Decomposes *cost accounts*.
- These three are cross-referenced through a **Responsibility Assignment Matrix (RAM / RACI chart)**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Tender Stages vs. WBS Integration

| Tender Stage | Planning Output Required (WBS-derived) |
|---|---|
| Stage 1: Need ID | High-level WBS (Level 1–2) |
| Stage 2: Document Prep | Detailed WBS (Level 3–4) → BOQ, Specifications |
| Stage 3: Publication | WBS-coded tender notice |
| Stage 4: Bidding | Contractor pricing against WBS work packages |
| Stage 5: Evaluation | WBS-linked comparative analysis |
| Stage 6: Award | WBS used as basis for *Schedule of Work* and *Milestone Payment Schedule* |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Process Flows & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Tendering Process for a Kerala PWD Building Project

> [!NOTE]
> This exhaustive walkthrough demonstrates the *practical* application of tendering theory in a *Kerala PWD-type* public works project, with a project value of **₹15 Crores (Civil + Electrical)**.

**Step 1: Project Inception**
The user department (e.g., District Hospital, Kottayam) submits a *demand note* with cost estimate (₹15 Cr) prepared as per *DSR (Delhi Schedule of Rates) + Cost Index* applicable to Kerala.

**Step 2: Administrative Sanction**
The competent authority (Secretary / Minister, depending on cost slab) issues *Government Order (GO)* with *Administrative Sanction* — the formal budget approval.

**Step 3: Technical Sanction**
The Chief Engineer / Superintending Engineer issues *Technical Sanction* after detailed scrutiny of design, drawings, and estimates.

**Step 4: Detailed Estimate Preparation**
Quantity Surveyor (AEE) prepares:
- Quantity takeoff
- Rate analysis (DSR + market rates)
- Detailed estimate: ~₹15 Cr
- *Atypical items* (if any) added with supporting analysis.

**Step 5: Preparation of Tender Documents**
Drafted by AE/AEE, vetted by EE, approved by CE:
- NIT (Notice Inviting Tender)
- Schedule A (Scope), Schedule B (BOQ), Schedule C (Specifications)
- General Conditions (PWD Manual / CPWD GCC)
- Special Conditions
- Draft Agreement
- List of Approved Makes

**Step 6: Tender Publication**
NIT published in:
- Two leading newspapers (one Malayalam, one English) — mandatory for tenders > ₹5 lakh.
- State Public Works Department website.
- e-Procurement portal (CPPP / Kerala e-tender).
- Notice Board of concerned Division.

**Step 7: Sale of Tender Documents**
Period: typically **30 days** for works > ₹50 lakh (as per Kerala Tenders Rules).
Price of document: ~ ₹5,000 + GST.
EMD: ~ ₹15,00,000 (1% of estimated cost).

**Step 8: Pre-Bid Meeting**
Held at the office of the Superintending Engineer.
Minutes issued as **Corrigendum** — legally binding addendum to tender.

**Step 9: Receipt of Bids**
Sealed tenders received up to stipulated date/time.
Late bids: rejected outright.

**Step 10: Tender Opening**
- **Technical bid** opened first (Two-envelope system).
- Online tenders opened in *e-procurement platform* with auto-decryption.
- Tender Opening Register (Form K-2 / equivalent) signed by all present.

**Step 11: Technical Evaluation**
Bid responsiveness checked:
- EMD valid?
- Power of Attorney submitted?
- Similar work experience criteria met?
- Litigation history disclosed?
- Solvency certificate valid?

**Step 12: Financial Evaluation**
Financial bid of *technically qualified* bidders opened.
*Comparative Statement* prepared → L1 identified.
Negotiations (if permitted) → Award recommended.

**Step 13: Issue of Letter of Acceptance (LOA)**
Issued by competent authority to L1 contractor.
LOA is **legally binding** under Indian Contract Act, 1872.

**Step 14: Performance Security Deposit**
Contractor deposits **5%–10% of contract value** (typically 5% for PWD works) within **14–28 days**.

**Step 15: Agreement Signing**
Formal contract agreement executed on **non-judicial stamp paper** of appropriate value (as per Kerala Stamp Act).

**Step 16: Notice to Proceed (NTP) / Work Order**
Issued → Contractor mobilizes → Project execution begins.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: WBS Development for a Building Project

> [!NOTE]
> Example: *Construction of a 4-storey Academic Block (G+3) for an Engineering College in Kerala — 2,500 m² built-up area.*

**Step 1: Define Project Scope Statement**
"Design and construct a G+3 academic block of 2,500 m² including civil, electrical, plumbing, and fire-fighting works, completed in 18 months at a contract value of ₹8 Cr."

**Step 2: Identify Major Deliverables (Level 1)**
1. Pre-Construction Activities
2. Substructure
3. Superstructure
4. MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing) Services
5. Finishes & Interiors
6. External Development
7. Project Management & Closeout

**Step 3: Decompose to Level 2 (Major Sub-Deliverables)**
- Substructure: Site Clearance, Excavation, Foundation, Plinth Beam
- Superstructure: Columns, Beams, Slabs, Staircase
- MEP: Electrical HT/LT, Plumbing, Fire-Fighting, HVAC
- Finishes: Flooring, Plastering, Painting, Joinery

**Step 4: Decompose to Level 3 (Work Packages)**
- Foundation → Open/Pile Foundation (each pile = a work package)
- Columns → Column up to Plinth, Column G+1, Column G+2, Column G+3
- Slabs → Slab at each floor level

**Step 5: Decompose to Level 4 (Activities)**
- Column Casting → Shuttering, Reinforcement placement, Concreting, Curing, Stripping
- *Each activity must be measurable in terms of quantity, time, and cost.*

**Step 6: Assign WBS Codes (Numerically)**
Example coding scheme:
- 1.0 = Pre-Construction
- 2.0 = Substructure
- 2.1 = Site Clearance
- 2.2 = Excavation
- 2.2.1 = Excavation in Soft Rock
- 2.2.2 = Excavation in Hard Rock
- 2.3 = Foundation
- 2.3.1 = Pile Cap — Pile P-1
- 2.3.2 = Pile Cap — Pile P-2
- ... and so on.

**Step 7: Develop WBS Dictionary**
For each *leaf-level work package*, the dictionary specifies:
- Description
- Responsible organization (OBS linkage)
- Estimated cost (CBS linkage)
- Estimated duration
- Acceptance criteria
- Resource requirements

**Step 8: Validate 100% Rule**
Sum of all Level-4 work package costs = Total project cost (₹8 Cr ± 2% contingency).

Verification equation:

$$C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{i}^{WP} \pm C_{contingency} \pm C_{escalation}$$

**Step 9: Establish Control Accounts**
Each Control Account (CA) = a *node* in the WBS that is used for:
- Earned Value Management (EVM)
- Cost Performance Index (CPI) tracking
- Schedule Performance Index (SPI) tracking

**Step 10: Integrate with Schedule (CPM/PERT)**
Each WBS leaf node becomes an *activity* in the project network diagram, with logical sequencing (FS, SS, FF, SF relationships).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Sequential Flow of the Six Stages of Tendering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1: Project Need ID and Feasibility] --> A2[Stage 2: Tender Document Preparation]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3: Tender Publication and Sale]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4: Bid Submission and Pre-Bid]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5: Bid Evaluation]
    A5 --> A6[Stage 6: Award of Contract and Agreement]
    A6 --> A7[Project Execution Phase Begins]

    A1 -- Administrative Sanction --> A2
    A2 -- NIT published --> A3
    A3 -- Sealed Bids received --> A4
    A4 -- Comparative Statement --> A5
    A5 -- Letter of Acceptance --> A6

    style A1 fill:#fff4cc
    style A2 fill:#cce5ff
    style A3 fill:#d4edda
    style A4 fill:#f8d7da
    style A5 fill:#e2d4f0
    style A6 fill:#ffd6a5
    style A7 fill:#b5e7a0
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical WBS for a G+3 Building Project

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[1.0 PROJECT - G plus 3 Academic Block]
    P0 --> L1A[1.1 Pre-Construction]
    P0 --> L1B[1.2 Substructure]
```
