<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Define the term "Material Schedule". List any four objectives of preparing a material schedule in a construction project.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
A Material Schedule is a comprehensive, time-phased document that itemizes all construction materials with their **type, quantity, unit, rate, lead time, and required delivery date**, derived from drawings, BOQ, and the project schedule, used for procurement and inventory control.

**Four Objectives (1.5 Marks — any four, 0.375 each):**
1. To enable systematic procurement planning aligned with the construction schedule.
2. To optimize inventory carrying and ordering costs through EOQ-based ordering.
3. To forecast material costs and support project cash flow management.
4. To ensure uninterrupted site operations by preventing material stock-outs.
5. To facilitate vendor management and competitive tendering.
6. To minimize wastage by providing accurate quantity take-offs.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supple Exam]**
> **Explain the concept of "Safety Stock" in material scheduling. State the formula for safety stock under variable demand and constant lead time.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Concept (1.5 Marks):**
Safety Stock (SS) is the **buffer inventory** maintained to protect against uncertainties in demand (consumption rate) and supply (lead time). It acts as a cushion so that the project does not face a stock-out during the lead time period.

**Formula (1.5 Marks):**

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

Where:
- $Z$ = Standard normal variate for the desired service level (e.g., 1.65 for 95%, 2.33 for 99%)
- $\sigma_{d}$ = Standard deviation of daily demand
- $L$ = Lead time in days

> A higher service level (e.g., 99%) requires a higher Z-value, leading to a larger safety stock but fewer stock-out incidents.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 1, Q1a/1b Style]**
>
> **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain in detail the various steps involved in the preparation of a Material Schedule for a construction project. List the documents required at each step. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The preparation of a material schedule is a **systematic 8-step process** that integrates design, commercial, and scheduling inputs:

**Step 1 — Study of Drawings and Specifications (1 Mark)**
- **Documents Required:** GFC (Good for Construction) drawings, technical specifications, special notes, IS codes.
- The estimator extracts material types, grades, dimensions, and finishing requirements from architectural, structural, and MEP drawings.

**Step 2 — Quantity Take-off from BOQ (1 Mark)**
- **Documents Required:** Draft Bill of Quantities, IS 1200 measurement sheets.
- Quantities are measured using standard methods (e.g., concrete in m³, steel in kg, plaster in m²). Deductions are made as per IS 1200 (e.g., >0.3 m² openings in plaster).

**Step 3 — Application of Wastage Factors (1 Mark)**
- **Documents Required:** Wastage allowance table (IS 1200 + KTU standard).
- Standard wastage is added: cement 2%, steel 3%, aggregates 5%, tiles 5%.

**Step 4 — Activity-wise Mapping with CPM/PERT Schedule (1 Mark)**
- **Documents Required:** Project schedule, Gantt chart, network diagram.
- Each material is mapped to the activity that consumes it. For example, cement for plastering is linked to the plastering activity's start and end dates.

**Step 5 — Lead Time Determination (1 Mark)**
- **Documents Required:** Vendor catalogues, historical procurement data.
- Lead times: cement 3–5 days, steel 7–15 days, RMC 1 day, façade 60–90 days.

**Step 6 — Backward Delivery Date Calculation (1 Mark)**
- **Documents Required:** Material schedule draft.
- `Delivery Date = Activity Start Date − Lead Time − Safety Buffer`.

**Step 7 — Application of Inventory Models (0.5 Mark)**
- EOQ is calculated for high-value materials; safety stock and ROP are determined.

**Step 8 — Approval and PR Issuance (0.5 Mark)**
- **Documents Required:** Approved material schedule, Purchase Requisition form.
- The schedule is signed by the Project Manager and sent to procurement for RFQ.

---

**(b) The annual demand for TMT steel bars in a construction project is 800 tonnes. The ordering cost is Rs. 5,000 per order, and the holding cost is Rs. 250 per tonne per year. Calculate the EOQ, number of orders per year, time between orders (in months), and the minimum total inventory cost. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 800$ tonnes/year
- $S = Rs.\ 5{,}000$/order
- $H = Rs.\ 250$/tonne/year

**Step 1 — Economic Order Quantity (2 Marks)**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 800 \times 5{,}000}{250}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{8{,}000{,}000}{250}} = \sqrt{32{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{EOQ \approx 178.89 \approx 179 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Step 2 — Number of Orders per Year (1.5 Marks)**

$$N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{800}{178.89}$$

$$\boxed{N = 4.47 \approx 5 \text{ orders/year}}$$

**Step 3 — Time Between Orders (1.5 Marks)**

$$TBO = \frac{12 \text{ months}}{N} = \frac{12}{4.47}$$

$$\boxed{TBO \approx 2.68 \text{ months}}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Total Inventory Cost (2 Marks)**

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 800 \times 5{,}000 \times 250}$$

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2{,}000{,}000{,}000} = 44{,}721.36$$

$$\boxed{TC^{*} \approx Rs.\ 44{,}721/\text{year}}$$

> *Conversion logic:* We used the derived minimum cost formula. Note that ordering cost equals holding cost at optimum: $AOC = AHC = Rs.\ 22{,}361$.

---

> **PART B — QUESTION B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)**
>
> **(a) Explain ABC analysis of inventory with a suitable example. How is it used in material scheduling of construction projects? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of ABC Analysis (2 Marks):**

ABC analysis is an inventory classification technique based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**. Materials are classified into three categories based on their **annual consumption value** (Quantity × Unit Price):

| Category | % of Items | % of Annual Value | Control Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 10%–20% | 70%–80% | Tight (daily monitoring, EOQ, strict records) |
| **B** | 30% | 15%–25% | Moderate (weekly review, periodic ordering) |
| **C** | 50% | 5%–10% | Loose (bulk ordering, minimal records) |

**Example from a Construction Project (3 Marks):**

Consider a residential building project with 100 line items in the BOQ:
- **A items (12 items):** TMT steel, cement, RMC, structural steel — these 12 items account for 75% of material cost. → **Tight control with EOQ, ROP, safety stock**.
- **B items (28 items):** Bricks, tiles, sanitary fixtures, plumbing pipes — 20% of cost. → **Moderate control**.
- **C items (60 items):** Nails, putty, sand paper, small hardware — 5% of cost. → **Bulk purchase once, loose records**.

**Use in Material Scheduling (2 Marks):**
1. **A items** dominate cash flow → apply EOQ rigorously, use just-in-time (JIT) delivery to minimize carrying cost.
2. **B items** use periodic review systems (e.g., monthly ordering) with moderate safety stock.
3. **C items** are ordered in bulk to reduce ordering frequency and administrative effort.
4. Helps procurement allocate **vendor negotiation effort** wisely — focus on A items for price discounts.
5. Reduces **inventory management effort by 60%–70%** while retaining 95% of cost control.

---

**(b) A project consumes 2,000 bags of cement per month. The standard deviation of daily demand is 15 bags. The lead time is 6 days, and the desired service level is 95% (Z = 1.65). Calculate the Safety Stock and the Reorder Point. Assume 30 working days per month. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Monthly demand = 2,000 bags → Daily demand $d = \frac{2{,}000}{30} = 66.67$ bags/day
- $\sigma_{d} = 15$ bags/day
- Lead time $L = 6$ days
- $Z = 1.65$ (for 95% service level)
- Assume SS is the only buffer

**Step 1 — Calculate Safety Stock (3.5 Marks)**

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

$$SS = 1.65 \times 15 \times \sqrt{6}$$

$$SS = 1.65 \times 15 \times 2.449$$

$$SS = 60.62 \text{ bags}$$

$$\boxed{SS \approx 61 \text{ bags}}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The standard deviation of demand during lead time is $\sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ because variance adds up over independent daily periods. We then multiply by the Z-score to get the buffer for the chosen service level.

**Step 2 — Calculate Reorder Point (3.5 Marks)**

$$ROP = d \cdot L + SS$$

$$ROP = 66.67 \times 6 + 60.62$$

$$ROP = 400.02 + 60.62$$

$$\boxed{ROP \approx 461 \text{ bags}}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The reorder point must cover the expected demand during lead time (400 bags) **plus** the safety buffer (61 bags). When inventory falls to 461 bags, a new order must be triggered.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing up BOQ and Material Schedule:** Many students write material schedule = BOQ in Part A. KTU deducts **1 mark** for this conceptual error.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply wastage:** In numerical problems, students often compute the BOQ quantity and use it directly. Always add **wastage %** before computing the cost. **−1 to −2 marks penalty.**
> 3. **Confusing variable demand and variable lead time safety stock formulas:** The two formulas look similar. **Memorize:** if demand varies → $\sigma_{d}\sqrt{L}$; if lead time varies → $\sigma_{L} \cdot d$.
> 4. **Wrong units in numerical answers:** EOQ is in *the same unit as demand* (e.g., bags, tonnes, m³). TBO can be in days OR months — **specify the unit** or the examiner deducts 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Not verifying AOC = AHC at optimum:** This is a **2-mark theory question** frequently asked. Always state and verify this property in your EOQ solutions.
> 6. **Skipping the lead time off-set in scheduling questions:** When asked to find the "delivery date," students forget to subtract lead time. **−2 marks.**
> 7. **Forgetting to state assumptions of EOQ model:** Constant demand, constant lead time, instantaneous replenishment, no stock-outs — these assumptions must be mentioned for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Material Schedule**

- **Definition:** A time-phased document listing *what* materials, *how much*, *when* to deliver, and at *what* cost for a construction project.
- **Difference from BOQ:** BOQ is *commercial* (quantity + rate); Material Schedule is *managerial* (quantity + time + logistics).
- **Types:** Master / Stage-wise / Activity-wise / Vendor-wise material schedules.
- **Preparation Steps (8):** Drawing study → BOQ takeoff → IS 1200 measurement → Wastage → CPM mapping → Lead time → Backward delivery date → EOQ/ROP/SS → PR issuance.
- **Standard Wastage:** Cement 2%, Steel 3%, Aggregates 5–7.5%, Bricks 5%, Tiles 5–10%, Timber 10%.
- **EOQ Formula:** $EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ — Wilson's Square Root Formula.
- **Minimum TC:** $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ — and at this point **AOC = AHC** (memorize this property).
- **Number of Orders:** $N = D / EOQ$.
- **Time Between Orders:** $TBO = 12/N$ months or $365/N$ days.
- **Reorder Point:** $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$.
- **Safety Stock (variable demand):** $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L}$.
- **Safety Stock (variable lead time):** $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{L} \cdot d$.
- **ABC Analysis:** A = 10–20% items / 70–80% value → tight control. C = 50% items / 5–10% value → loose control.
- **Lead Time Off-set:** $T_{delivery} = T_{activity\,start} - L - B$ (always subtract lead time and buffer).
- **Standard Units:** Cement in bags/tonnes, Steel in kg/tonnes, Concrete in m³, Bricks in Nos.
- **Cost Share:** Materials = **60–70%** of total project cost — hence material schedule is the most critical management document after the project schedule itself.
- **Assumptions of EOQ:** Constant demand rate, constant lead time, instantaneous replenishment, no quantity discounts, independent ordering and holding costs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Labour Schedule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET604 - CONSTRUCTION PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Labour Schedule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Labour Schedule (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *Labour Schedule* in construction project management is a **time-phased, quantitative deployment plan** that specifies the **number, type, skill category, and timing of construction workers (manpower)** required to execute each scheduled activity of a project, ensuring that the workforce is available **in the right quantity, at the right time, with the right skills**, without over-allocation (peak demand surge) or under-allocation (idle workforce).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), the Labour Schedule is classified as a **derived sub-schedule of the Master Resource Schedule**, and is produced *after* the **Construction Schedule (Time Schedule / Bar Chart / CPM / PERT)** has been finalized. It acts as the bridge between the *time domain* (when activities occur) and the *resource domain* (who executes them).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy"

Imagine you are running a busy restaurant kitchen preparing for dinner service:
- The **menu** = the Bill of Quantities (BOQ) of the project.
- The **order tickets** = construction activities (slab casting, plastering, etc.).
- The **chefs and helpers** = different categories of labour (masons, helpers, bar benders, carpenters).
- The **kitchen schedule** (who starts cooking what at what time) = the **Labour Schedule**.

If too many chefs arrive at 9 AM and nothing happens later, the restaurant wastes money (this is **over-allocation**). If only one chef is available when 50 orders arrive, the kitchen collapses (this is **under-allocation / resource starvation**). The Labour Schedule is the manager's tool to keep staffing **smooth, balanced, and cost-efficient** — exactly as a *histogram* in project management terms.

### 1.3 Why Labour Scheduling is a KTU High-Weightage Topic

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Significance:**
> - It directly supports **Course Outcome CO1 / CO2** of PBCET604: *Plan, schedule, and control construction resources*.
> - It links to **Bloom's Cognitive Levels L3 (Apply) and L4 (Analyze)** — KTU frequently tests numerical problems (e.g., calculating crew size, preparing histograms).
> - Industry relevance: ~30–40% of project cost in India is **direct labour cost**, making this schedule vital for cost control.

### 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics

The following standard KTU-recommended metrics govern labour scheduling:

- **Standard Working Hours:** **8 hours/day** and **48 hours/week** (per Indian Factory Act, 1948 — adopted by KTU reference texts).
- **Unit of Measurement:** *Man-hours* (1 worker × 1 hour) or *Man-days* (1 worker × 8 hours).
- **Daily Output Constants:** Tabulated in textbooks as **Standard Task / Daily Output** values (e.g., a mason lays 1.0 m³ of brickwork/day).
- **Wastage / Idle Factor:** Generally **10–20%** added to theoretical labour requirement.
- **Absenteeism Buffer:** **15%** (typical KTU assumption).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of Preparing a Labour Schedule

The Labour Schedule is prepared to satisfy **five primary objectives**:

1. **Ensure Availability:** Guarantee that the required workforce is on-site when the activity starts.
2. **Minimize Idle Time:** Avoid over-manning (workers sitting without work).
3. **Smooth Resource Demand:** Reduce peaks and valleys in the labour histogram (resource leveling).
4. **Cost Optimization:** Direct labour often constitutes the largest controllable cost (~25–35% of total project cost in India).
5. **Skill Matching:** Deploy the correct *trade* (mason, carpenter, plumber, electrician) to the correct activity.

### 2.2 Inputs Required to Prepare a Labour Schedule

| S.No. | Input Document | Purpose |
|-------|----------------|---------|
| 1 | **Construction Schedule** (Bar Chart / CPM) | Defines *when* each activity is planned |
| 2 | **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** | Defines *how much* work is to be done |
| 3 | **Daily Output Constants** (Standard Task) | Defines *how fast* a worker works |
| 4 | **Activity Duration** | Defines the *time window* for deployment |
| 5 | **Wastage & Absenteeism Factor** | Defines a *safety buffer* |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for Labour Schedule (KTU Exam Ready)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{Q}{P \times D}$ | Number of workers required | persons |
| 2 | $N_{actual} = N \times (1 + W + A)$ | Workers with wastage & absenteeism | persons |
| 3 | $MH = N \times h \times D$ | Total man-hours required | man-hours |
| 4 | $MD = N \times D$ | Total man-days required | man-days |
| 5 | $C_{labour} = MH \times R$ | Total labour cost | Rupees |
| 6 | $P = \dfrac{Q}{N \times D}$ | Productivity per worker per day | units/day |
| 7 | $L_{hist} = \sum N_i$ for activities in day $i$ | Histogram bar height for day $i$ | persons |

**Where:**
- $Q$ = Quantity of work (e.g., m³, m², kg, nos.)
- $P$ = Daily output per worker (productivity)
- $D$ = Duration of activity (days)
- $h$ = Hours worked per day (typically **8**)
- $W$ = Wastage factor (decimal, e.g., 0.10 for 10%)
- $A$ = Absenteeism factor (decimal, e.g., 0.15 for 15%)
- $R$ = Wage rate per hour (₹/hour)
- $N$ = Number of workers
- $MH$ = Man-hours
- $MD$ = Man-days
- $L_{hist}$ = Histogram bar value (number of labourers on a given day)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Utility |
|-------------|---------|
| **Site Mobilization Planning** | Decide when to hire/fire workers and arrange labour camps |
| **Cash Flow Forecasting** | Predict weekly/monthly wage outflows |
| **Resource Leveling** | Smooth peaks to avoid over-crowding and accidents |
| **Procurement Linkage** | Align material delivery with workforce readiness |
| **Safety Management** | Prevent overcrowding at work fronts |
| **Contractor Billing** | RA (Running Account) bills depend on actual labour deployed |

### 2.5 Types of Labour Schedules (KTU 2024 Classification)

1. **Resource Histogram (Bar Chart of Labour):** Bar graph of manning level vs. time.
2. **Resource Aggregation Schedule:** Sum of overlapping activity requirements.
3. **Resource-Levelled Schedule:** Modified schedule that *flattens* the histogram by extending activity durations.
4. **Cumulative Labour (S-Curve):** Shows progressive man-hours deployed.
5. **Crew-based Schedule:** Deploys predefined crews (e.g., 1 mason + 2 helpers) to activities.

### 2.6 Difference Between Resource Aggregation and Resource Leveling

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:**

| Aspect | Resource Aggregation | Resource Leveling |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Activity Duration | **Unchanged** | **May be extended** |
| Start/Finish Dates | **Unchanged** | **Shifted (within float)** |
| Objective | Quantify total demand | Smooth demand (flatter histogram) |
| Project End Date | Unchanged | May be extended (in time-limited mode) |
| Algorithm | Simple summation | Heuristic / Integer programming |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation of the Core Crew Size Formula

**Derivation:** *Given a construction activity of quantity $Q$ units, productivity $P$ units per worker per day, and available duration $D$ days, find the crew size $N$.*

**Step 1:** Define the relationship.

$$
\text{Work Done} = (\text{Number of Workers}) \times (\text{Daily Output per Worker}) \times (\text{Number of Days})
$$

**Step 2:** Express in symbols.

$$
Q = N \times P \times D
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for $N$ (the unknown crew size).

$$
N = \frac{Q}{P \times D}
$$

**Step 4:** Account for realistic site conditions — wastage $W$ and absenteeism $A$.

$$
N_{actual} = \frac{Q}{P \times D} \times (1 + W + A)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The raw formula gives the *theoretical* minimum. Multiplying by $(1+W+A)$ inflates the crew to cover lost hours due to wastage (rework, idle material) and absenteeism (sick leave, festivals).

---

### 3.2 Solved Example 1 — Basic Crew Size Calculation (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** A masonry activity involves laying **500 m³** of brickwork. The standard daily output of a mason is **1.25 m³/day**. The activity is scheduled to run for **40 days**. The foreman-to-mason ratio is **1:10** (i.e., 1 foreman for every 10 masons). Find:
> (a) Number of masons required.
> (b) Total number of workers (including foremen).

**Given:**
- $Q = 500 \text{ m}^3$
- $P = 1.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{day/mason}$
- $D = 40 \text{ days}$

**Step 1 — Calculate theoretical masons.**

$$
N = \frac{Q}{P \times D} = \frac{500}{1.25 \times 40}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate denominator.**

$$
1.25 \times 40 = 50
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
N = \frac{500}{50} = 10 \text{ masons}
$$

**Step 4 — Add foremen at 1:10 ratio.**

$$
\text{Foremen} = \frac{10}{10} = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Total workforce.**

$$
N_{total} = 10 + 1 = 11 \text{ workers}
$$

**Answer:** *10 masons + 1 foreman = 11 workers.*

---

### 3.3 Solved Example 2 — With Wastage and Absenteeism (KTU Advanced)

> **Problem:** For the same activity, apply a wastage factor of **10%** and absenteeism of **15%**. Find the *actual* number of masons to be deployed.

**Step 1 — Apply the buffer multiplier.**

$$
N_{actual} = N \times (1 + W + A) = 10 \times (1 + 0.10 + 0.15)
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the factors.**

$$
1 + 0.10 + 0.15 = 1.25
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
N_{actual} = 10 \times 1.25 = 12.5
$$

**Step 4 — Round up (cannot employ half a worker).**

$$
N_{actual} = 13 \text{ masons}
$$

**Step 5 — Add 2 foremen (re-evaluated at 1:10 ratio of 13 = ⌈13/10⌉).**

$$
N_{total} = 13 + 2 = 15 \text{ workers}
$$

**Answer:** *13 masons + 2 foremen = 15 workers.*

---

### 3.4 Solved Example 3 — Man-Hours and Labour Cost

> **Problem:** A concreting activity requires **20 workers** working for **12 days**, **8 hours/day**. The wage rate is **₹85/hour**. Calculate (a) total man-hours and (b) total labour cost.

**Step 1 — Total man-hours.**

$$
MH = N \times h \times D = 20 \times 8 \times 12
$$

**Step 2 — Compute.**

$$
MH = 1920 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Total labour cost.**

$$
C_{labour} = MH \times R = 1920 \times 85
$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$
C_{labour} = \text{₹}1{,}63{,}200
$$

**Answer:** *1920 man-hours; ₹1,63,200.*

---

### 3.5 Solved Example 4 — Resource Aggregation & Histogram (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** Three activities overlap as follows. Compute the labour histogram for Days 1 to 8.

| Activity | Duration | Workers Required |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| A | Days 1–4 | 10 |
| B | Days 3–6 | 15 |
| C | Days 5–8 | 8 |

**Step 1 — Tabulate daily demand (Aggregation = sum of active activities).**

| Day | A | B | C | Total |
|-----|---|---|---|-------|
| 1   | 10 | 0 | 0 | **10** |
| 2   | 10 | 0 | 0 | **10** |
| 3   | 10 | 15 | 0 | **25** |
| 4   | 10 | 15 | 0 | **25** |
| 5   | 0 | 15 | 8 | **23** |
| 6   | 0 | 15 | 8 | **23** |
| 7   | 0 | 0 | 8 | **8**  |
| 8   | 0 | 0 | 8 | **8**  |

**Step 2 — Identify peak demand.**

$$
\text{Peak} = 25 \text{ workers (on Days 3 and 4)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute average demand.**

$$
\bar{N} = \frac{10+10+25+25+23+23+8+8}{8} = \frac{132}{8} = 16.5 \text{ workers}
$$

**Step 4 — Resource leveling (optional).**  
If project target is $\le 20$ workers, we can split Activity B by 1 day using its float to flatten the peak from 25 → 20. *(Detailed in SECTION_4 as a flow diagram.)*

**Answer:** *Peak = 25 workers; Average = 16.5 workers.*

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Formula

| Situation | Recommended Formula | Why |
|-----------|---------------------|-----|
| Estimate crew for a single activity | $N = \dfrac{Q}{P \times D}$ | Direct, simple |
| Account for site realities | $N \times (1 + W + A)$ | Includes buffer |
| Compute wage bill | $MH \times R$ | Standard payroll formula |
| Build a labour histogram | $\sum N_i$ per day | Aggregation logic |
| Smooth peaks | Activity splitting / float use | Resource leveling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Labour Scheduling Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Initiated] --> B[Prepare Construction Schedule CPM Bar Chart]
    B --> C[Extract Bill of Quantities BOQ per Activity]
    C --> D[List Daily Output Constants P per Trade]
    D --> E[Compute Theoretical Crew Size N = Q by P times D]
    E --> F[Apply Wastage Absenteeism Buffer]
    F --> G[Compute Actual Crew Size N_actual]
    G --> H[Plot Resource Histogram Daily Manning]
    H --> I{Is Peak Demand within Site Capacity?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Finalize Labour Schedule]
    I -- No --> K[Apply Resource Leveling using Float]
    K --> L[Reschedule Activities within Float]
    L --> H
    J --> M[Generate Labour Deployment Chart and S Curve]
    M --> N[End: Schedule Approved for Execution]
```

### 4.2 Resource Histogram Schematic (Text-Based Block Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Day1to8["LABOUR HISTOGRAM (Workers vs Time)"]
        direction LR
        D1["Day 1: |██████████ 10"]
        D2["Day 2: |██████████ 10"]
        D3["Day 3: |██████████████████████████ 25 PEAK"]
        D4["Day 4: |██████████████████████████ 25 PEAK"]
        D5["Day 5: |███████████████████████ 23"]
        D6["Day 6: |███████████████████████ 23"]
        D7["Day 7: |████████ 8"]
        D8["Day 8: |████████ 8"]
        D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5 --> D6 --> D7 --> D8
    end
```

### 4.3 Crew Composition Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Construction Project Workforce] --> B1[Skilled Workers]
    A1 --> B2[Semi-Skilled Workers]
    A1 --> B3[Unskilled Workers]
    A1 --> B4[Supervisory Staff]
    B1 --> C1[Masons Carpenters Bar Benders Electricians Plumbers]
    B2 --> C2[Helpers Masons Mate Tile Asst]
    B3 --> C3[Beldars Earth Movers Cleaners]
    B4 --> C4[Site Engineer Foreman Supervisor Safety Officer]
```

### 4.4 Resource Aggregation vs. Leveling (Comparison Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: CPM Network with Resources] --> B[Resource Aggregation]
    A --> C[Resource Leveling]
    B --> D[Total Manning per Day = Sum of Active Activities]
    C --> E[Adjust Start Finish within Float]
    D --> F[Histogram with Sharp Peaks]
    E --> G[Smoothed Histogram within Limit]
    F --> H[Use: Quick Estimation]
    G --> I[Use: Site Execution Planning]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**

**Q1. Define a Labour Schedule and state any two of its objectives.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A Labour Schedule is a time-phased deployment plan that specifies the number, type, and timing of construction workers required for each scheduled activity. It is derived from the construction schedule, the Bill of Quantities, and standard productivity data.

**Two objectives:**
1. To ensure the right number of skilled workers is available at the right time.
2. To minimize idle time and over-manning, thereby optimizing direct labour cost.

---

**Q2. What is a Resource Histogram? How is it different from a Cumulative Labour (S) Curve?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- A *Resource Histogram* is a bar chart that shows the **number of workers required on each day** of the project (i.e., daily manning).
- A *Cumulative Labour (S) Curve* shows the **cumulative sum of man-hours deployed** up to any given date, producing an "S-shaped" growth curve.
- The histogram shows instantaneous demand; the S-curve shows progressive deployment. Together they form the basis of the labour schedule.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the stepwise procedure to prepare a Labour Schedule for a construction project. List the inputs and outputs of each step. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A concreting activity involves placing **800 m³** of M25 concrete. The standard daily output per worker is **1.6 m³/day**. The scheduled duration is **25 days**. Apply a wastage factor of **12%** and absenteeism of **10%**. Calculate:
   (i) Theoretical number of workers.
   (ii) Actual number of workers to be deployed.
   (iii) Total man-hours if each works **8 hours/day**.
   (iv) Total labour cost at **₹95/hour**. *(7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

The procedure to prepare a Labour Schedule is as follows:

1. **Step 1 — Finalize the Construction Schedule:** Prepare the Bar Chart / CPM / PERT network. This gives start and finish dates for every activity.
2. **Step 2 — Extract the Bill of Quantities (BOQ):** List the quantity $Q$ of work for each activity (in m³, m², kg, etc.).
3. **Step 3 — Identify Daily Output Constants ($P$):** From standard task tables, list the daily output per worker for each trade.
4. **Step 4 — Compute Crew Size ($N$):** Apply the formula $N = \dfrac{Q}{P \times D}$.
5. **Step 5 — Apply Buffers:** Multiply by $(1 + W + A)$ to get the *actual* workforce.
6. **Step 6 — Aggregate Daily Demand:** Sum the crew sizes of all activities that overlap on a given day → produces the **Resource Histogram**.
7. **Step 7 — Level Resources (if required):** If the histogram peak exceeds the site capacity, reschedule activities using float.
8. **Step 8 — Plot the S-Curve:** Plot cumulative man-hours vs. time to get the labour deployment curve.

**Inputs:** Construction schedule, BOQ, productivity tables, duration, wastage/absenteeism factors.  
**Outputs:** Resource histogram, S-curve, daily labour deployment chart, labour cost estimate.

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Given:**
$Q = 800 \text{ m}^3$, $P = 1.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$, $D = 25 \text{ days}$, $W = 0.12$, $A = 0.10$, $h = 8 \text{ hr}$, $R = \text{₹}95/\text{hr}$.

**(i) Theoretical number of workers:**

$$
N = \frac{Q}{P \times D} = \frac{800}{1.6 \times 25} = \frac{800}{40} = 20 \text{ workers}
$$

**(ii) Actual number of workers:**

$$
N_{actual} = N \times (1 + W + A) = 20 \times (1 + 0.12 + 0.10) = 20 \times 1.22 = 24.4
$$

Rounded up:

$$
N_{actual} = 25 \text{ workers}
$$

**(iii) Total man-hours:**

$$
MH = N_{actual} \times h \times D = 25 \times 8 \times 25 = 5000 \text{ man-hours}
$$

**(iv) Total labour cost:**

$$
C_{labour} = MH \times R = 5000 \times 95 = \text{₹}4{,}75{,}000
$$

**Final Answer:** 20 (theoretical) → 25 (actual) workers; 5000 man-hours; ₹4,75,000.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Distinguish between **Resource Aggregation** and **Resource Leveling**. Explain the conditions under which each is applied. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Three activities overlap on a project as given below. Prepare the **Labour Histogram** and calculate the **average** and **peak** manning levels.

| Activity | Duration (Days) | Workers |
|----------|-----------------|---------|
| P | 1–5 | 12 |
| Q | 3–7 | 18 |
| R | 6–10 | 10 |

*(7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

| Aspect | Resource Aggregation | Resource Leveling |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Sum of worker requirements of all concurrent activities | Adjustment of activity timing to smooth resource demand |
| Duration | Activity durations unchanged | Activities may be extended up to available float |
| Start/Finish | Fixed | Shifted within float |
| Use | Quantify demand; cost estimation | Match supply limits; reduce idle time |
| Output | Resource Histogram (with peaks) | Smoothed Histogram (with cap on max workers) |
| Mode | Unconstrained | Resource-constrained scheduling |

**Conditions for Aggregation:** When the only objective is to *know* the total manpower needed (e.g., for preliminary cost estimation or workforce mobilization).  
**Conditions for Leveling:** When the site has a *fixed capacity* (e.g., labour camp of 25 workers) or when the contractor wishes to avoid hiring/firing cycles.

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — Tabulate daily demand (Aggregation):**

| Day | P (12) | Q (18) | R (10) | Total |
|-----|--------|--------|--------|-------|
| 1   | 12     | 0      | 0      | **12** |
| 2   | 12     | 0      | 0      | **12** |
| 3   | 12     | 18     | 0      | **30** |
| 4   | 12     | 18     | 0      | **30** |
| 5   | 12     | 18     | 0      | **30** |
| 6   | 0      | 18     | 10     | **28** |
| 7   | 0      | 18     | 10     | **28** |
| 8   | 0      | 0      | 10     | **10** |
| 9   | 0      | 0      | 10     | **10** |
| 10  | 0      | 0      | 10     | **10** |

**Step 2 — Identify Peak:**

$$
\text{Peak} = 30 \text{ workers (Days 3, 4, 5)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Average:**

$$
\bar{N} = \frac{12+12+30+30+30+28+28+10+10+10}{10} = \frac{200}{10} = 20 \text{ workers}
$$

**Step 4 — Histogram (Bar chart description):**

- Days 1–2: 12 workers
- Days 3–5: 30 workers (peak)
- Days 6–7: 28 workers
- Days 8–10: 10 workers

**Final Answer:** Peak = 30 workers; Average = 20 workers; Histogram plotted as above.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round up** the actual workforce (e.g., 24.4 → 25, not 24). Half a worker cannot be employed — always **ceiling** the value.
> 2. **Mixing up the units of $P$** — some textbooks give $P$ as *units per hour*, others as *units per day*. Read the problem carefully.
> 3. **Confusing Man-hours with Man-days** — Man-hours = workers × hours/day × days. Man-days = workers × days.
> 4. **Ignoring wastage + absenteeism** in the final answer — KTU specifically tests this; 70% of past failures stem from this oversight.
> 5. **Resource leveling question trap:** Students often "level" by reducing crew size, which is wrong — leveling is done by **shifting activities within float**, not by under-manning.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Fire):**

- ✅ Labour Schedule = *Time-phased* deployment of manpower derived from the **Construction Schedule + BOQ + Productivity Data**.
- ✅ The **core formula** is: $N = \dfrac{Q}{P \times D}$.
- ✅ **Buffer formula:** $N_{actual} = N \times (1 + W + A)$.
- ✅ **Man-hours:** $MH = N \times h \times D$.
- ✅ **Labour cost:** $C = MH \times R$.
- ✅ **Resource Histogram** = bar chart of daily workers; **S-Curve** = cumulative man-hours.
- ✅ **Resource Aggregation** = sum of overlapping activity demands (durations unchanged).
- ✅ **Resource Leveling** = smooth peaks by rescheduling within float (durations may extend).
- ✅ **Peak demand** governs camp size, hiring, and site congestion.
- ✅ **Average demand** is used for long-term workforce planning.
- ✅ **Standard working hours:** 8 hr/day, 48 hr/week (Indian Factory Act).
- ✅ **Typical buffers:** W = 10–15%, A = 10–15%.
- ✅ **Always round up** crew size to the next whole number.
- ✅ **Crew composition** typically follows a 1:10 ratio of foreman to skilled workers.
- ✅ Labour cost ≈ 25–35% of total project cost in Indian construction.
- ✅ KTU pattern: Numerical problems on $N$ and $N_{actual}$ are worth 7 marks; Histogram problems are worth 7 marks — combined into a 14-mark Part B question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment Schedule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET604 - CONSTRUCTION PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equipment Schedule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Equipment Schedule – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equipment Schedule** is a comprehensive, time-phased planning document that systematically lists, organizes, and coordinates all construction machinery, equipment, tools, and plant required for the execution of a construction project. It defines **equipment type, capacity, quantity, deployment timing, productivity targets, hourly cost, source of procurement, and total utilization duration**, serving as a control instrument linking project schedule (CPM/PERT), cost estimate (BOQ), and resource allocation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Equipment Schedule is a sub-deliverable of the **Construction Resource Planning (CRP)** process and is tightly integrated with the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**, the **Project Network (CPM)**, and the **Project Cost Control System**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Equipment Schedule is the "Battlefield Deployment Chart" of a project manager.**

Imagine a **General commanding a large army operation**. Before moving troops, the General prepares a **Deployment Chart** that specifies:
- *Which regiment (equipment type)* – e.g., 1st Armoured (Excavator)
- *How many soldiers/vehicles (quantity)* – e.g., 4 tanks
- *When they move (timing)* – e.g., Day 3, 06:00 hrs
- *How long they stay (duration)* – e.g., 15 days
- *What supplies they need (fuel, operator, spares)*
- *Their daily output target (productivity)*

A construction project is exactly the same. The **Equipment Schedule** is the General's chart – it tells the site engineer *which machine, when, where, for how long, doing what, and at what cost*. Without it, machines arrive late, sit idle, clash with other activities, or are over-ordered, blowing up the budget.

## 1.3 Purpose & Importance

| S.No. | Purpose of Equipment Schedule | Engineering Significance |
|:-----:|:------------------------------|:--------------------------|
| 1 | **Resource leveling** across the project timeline | Avoids idle/over-utilized machines |
| 2 | **Cost estimation** – owns/operating cost per hour | Inputs to BOQ and project budget |
| 3 | **Coordination** between activities (excavation → hauling → placing) | Prevents bottlenecks |
| 4 | **Procurement planning** (own vs. rent vs. lease) | Reduces capital lock-up |
| 5 | **Productivity forecasting** (cycle time × efficiency) | Realistic work-front planning |
| 6 | **Cash flow scheduling** (rental vs. depreciation timing) | Aligns with finance milestones |
| 7 | **Safety & statutory compliance** (operator licensing, fitness certs) | KTU safety module linkage |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard working hours/month:** **200 hours (8 hr/day × 25 days)**
- **Standard working hours/year:** **~2,400 hours** for depreciation
- **1 hectare = 10,000 m²**
- **1 acre = 4,046.86 m²**
- **Standard earth weight (loose):** **1,400–1,600 kg/m³**
- **Standard earth weight (compacted/bank):** **1,800–2,000 kg/m³**
- **Swell factor (loose/bank):** typically **1.20 – 1.35**
- **Shrinkage factor (compacted/bank):** typically **0.85 – 0.95**
- **Bank measure → Loose measure:** Multiply by **Swell factor**
- **Heaped capacity of a dumper** is usually **Rated struck capacity × 1.20**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components (Anatomy) of an Equipment Schedule

A standard KTU-grade Equipment Schedule contains **10 mandatory columns**:

1. **S.No / Item Code** – Unique identifier
2. **Activity Reference** – Linked WBS/CPM Activity ID
3. **Equipment Description** – Type, model, capacity
4. **Specification** – Power, bucket size, payload
5. **Quantity (No.)** – Number required
6. **Productivity / Output** – m³/hr or tons/hr
7. **Deployment Window** – Start date to End date
8. **Duration (hours/days)** – Total utilization
9. **Hourly Rate (₹/hr)** – Owning + Operating cost
10. **Total Cost (₹)** – Quantity × Hours × Rate

> [!NOTE]
> An additional column called **Source** (Owned / Rented / Leased / Sub-contracted) is often added in industry practice to support procurement decisions.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Equipment Scheduling

The logic chain is:
**Project Work Quantities → Activity Duration → Machine Productivity → Number of Machines → Schedule Window → Hourly Cost → Total Equipment Cost**

- **Why schedule equipment?** Because *time = money*. Each idle hour of a 1 m³ excavator can cost **₹800–₹1,500** even when not working (standby cost).
- **How is it made?** By working *backward* from the activity deadline and *forward* from the activity start to determine the fleet size that satisfies both.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all formulas required for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on Equipment Schedule. Memorize these.

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Notation |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:----------------|
| 1 | **Loader Cycle Time** | $T_L = T_{fix,L} + \dfrac{q}{V_L}$ | min |
| 2 | **Hauler Cycle Time** | $T_H = T_{fix,H} + \dfrac{L}{V_{haul}} + \dfrac{L}{V_{return}}$ | min |
| 3 | **Loader Productivity** | $Q_L = \dfrac{60 \times q \times E}{T_L}$ | m³/hr (bank) |
| 4 | **Hauler Productivity** | $Q_H = \dfrac{60 \times C \times E}{T_H}$ | m³/hr (loose) |
| 5 | **Balanced Fleet Equation** | $n = \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} + 1$ | number of haulers per loader |
| 6 | **Swell Conversion** | $V_{loose} = V_{bank} \times S_f$ | m³ |
| 7 | **Shrinkage Conversion** | $V_{compacted} = V_{bank} \times S_h$ | m³ |
| 8 | **Equipment Owning Cost/hr** | $C_o = \dfrac{P - S}{H \times L} + \dfrac{(P+S)\times i}{2H} + \dfrac{I + T + St}{H}$ | ₹/hr |
| 9 | **Equipment Operating Cost/hr** | $C_{op} = F + L_b + M + O + W$ | ₹/hr |
| 10 | **Total Equipment Cost/hr** | $C = C_o + C_{op}$ | ₹/hr |
| 11 | **Number of Machines Required** | $N = \dfrac{\text{Total Quantity}}{\text{Daily Output} \times \text{Available Days}}$ | units |
| 12 | **Equipment Schedule Cost (Row)** | $R = N \times H_{used} \times C$ | ₹ |
| 13 | **Total Project Equipment Cost** | $E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> Where: $q$ = bucket/payload volume, $C$ = hauler capacity, $E$ = efficiency factor (0.75–0.85), $L$ = haul distance (m), $V_{haul},V_{return}$ = speeds loaded/empty, $P$ = purchase price, $S$ = salvage, $H$ = working hours/year, $L$ = life in years, $i$ = interest rate, $I$ = insurance, $T$ = taxes, $St$ = storage, $F$ = fuel, $L_b$ = lubricants, $M$ = maintenance, $O$ = operator wages, $W$ = wear (tires/tracks).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The Equipment Schedule feeds into:
- **Daily Site Log Book** – Each machine's hours run
- **Plant Management System** – Maintenance scheduling
- **Billing & Recovery** – Hire-charge claims in EPC contracts
- **Insurance & Statutory Returns** – Plant register
- **Contractor's Cash-Flow Curve** – Heavy outflow during mobilization
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Balanced Fleet Equation (Loader–Hauler Combination)

**Scenario:** One loader fills a fleet of identical haulers (trucks/dumpers) operating between an excavation pit and a fill/disposal area.

**Step 1 – Define Loader cycle:**
A loader performs: *Dig, Swing loaded, Dump into hauler, Swing empty.* This is captured in $T_L$ (in minutes).

$$
T_L = T_{fix,L} + \frac{q}{V_L}
$$

**Step 2 – Define Hauler cycle:**
A hauler performs: *Load (waits for loader), Haul, Dump, Return.*

$$
T_H = T_{fix,H} + \frac{L}{V_{haul}} + \frac{L}{V_{return}}
$$

**Step 3 – Equate Production Rates (Balance Condition):**
For the fleet to be balanced, the loader's output and the *combined* hauler fleet's output must match.

$$
Q_L = n \times Q_H
$$

**Step 4 – Substitute the productivity expressions:**

$$
\frac{60 \, q \, E_L}{T_L} = n \times \frac{60 \, C \, E_H}{T_H}
$$

**Step 5 – Simplify the constant terms (60 cancels on both sides):**

$$
n = \frac{q \, E_L \, T_H}{C \, E_H \, T_L}
$$

**Step 6 – Apply the standard assumption** that loader efficiency $E_L$ ≈ hauler efficiency $E_H$ and bucket-passes per hauler $C/q = k$ (an integer). This reduces to:

$$
n = \frac{T_H}{k \cdot T_L}
$$

**Step 7 – The "plus one" rule** arises from queuing theory: at any given instant, one hauler is *being loaded*; hence the *theoretical* number must be rounded up to the next whole number, then **+1** is added for the hauler currently *in* the loader's bucket.

$$
\boxed{n = \frac{T_H}{T_L} + 1}
$$

This is the **Peurifoy/Schexnayder formula** – the workhorse of KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of Owning Cost per Hour (Caterpillar/Peurifoy Model)

The owning cost (also called *fixed cost*) must be distributed over the equipment's economic working life.

**Step 1 – Depreciation per hour:**
Spread (Purchase − Salvage) over total working hours $H \times L$.

$$
D = \frac{P - S}{H \times L}
$$

**Step 2 – Average Investment Cost (Interest):**
Capital is tied up; charge interest on *average* invested value $(P+S)/2$.

$$
I_{hr} = \frac{(P+S)}{2} \times \frac{i}{H}
$$

**Step 3 – Insurance, Taxes, Storage per hour:**

$$
ITS_{hr} = \frac{I + T + St}{H}
$$

**Step 4 – Sum them all (Owning Cost/hr):**

$$
\boxed{C_o = \frac{P-S}{H \cdot L} + \frac{(P+S)\,i}{2H} + \frac{I+T+St}{H}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem:** A road project requires excavating **50,000 m³** of earth (bank measure) and hauling it an average **distance of 1.5 km** to a fill area. Available data:
> - Excavator: bucket $q = 1.5$ m³, cycle time $T_L = 0.4$ min, efficiency $E_L = 0.85$
> - Dumper: struck capacity $C = 6$ m³, speed loaded $V_h = 20$ km/h$, speed empty $V_r = 30$ km/h$
> - Dumper fixed time $T_{fix,H} = 1.5$ min, efficiency $E_H = 0.85$
> - Working: 8 hr/day, 25 days/month, must finish in **4 months**
> - Owning + Operating cost = ₹2,000/hr (loader), ₹1,500/hr (hauler)
>
> **Required:** (a) Balanced fleet; (b) Total project cost.

### Solution (a) – Balanced Fleet

**Step 1 – Dumper cycle time:**

$$
T_H = 1.5 + \frac{1.5 \times 60}{20} + \frac{1.5 \times 60}{30}
$$

(Convert km to minutes: 1.5 km at 20 km/h = 1.5/20 h = 4.5 min; 1.5 km at 30 km/h = 1.5/30 h = 3 min)

$$
T_H = 1.5 + 4.5 + 3.0 = 9.0 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 – Number of dumpers per loader:**

$$
n = \frac{9.0}{0.4} + 1 = 22.5 + 1 = 23.5 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{n = 24 \text{ dumpers}}
$$

### Solution (b) – Project Cost

**Step 3 – Total available hours:**

$$
H_{avail} = 4 \times 25 \times 8 = 800 \text{ hr}
$$

**Step 4 – Loader output (bank m³/hr):**

$$
Q_L = \frac{60 \times 1.5 \times 0.85}{0.4} = \frac{76.5}{0.4} = 191.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 5 – Required hourly output:**

$$
Q_{req} = \frac{50{,}000}{800} = 62.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 6 – Number of loaders required:**

$$
N_L = \frac{62.5}{191.25} = 0.327 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{N_L = 1 \text{ loader}}
$$

**Step 7 – Number of dumpers** (1 loader × 24 dumpers):

$$
N_H = 1 \times 24 = \boxed{24 \text{ dumpers}}
$$

**Step 8 – Total equipment cost:**

$$
C_{proj} = (1 \times 800 \times 2000) + (24 \times 800 \times 1500)
$$

$$
C_{proj} = 16{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}88{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{₹\,3{,}04{,}00{,}000}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Equipment Schedule Template (Filled Example)

| S.No | Activity | Equipment | Spec | Qty | Prod. (m³/hr) | Start | End | Hrs | Rate (₹/hr) | Cost (₹) |
|:----:|:---------|:----------|:-----|:----|:--------------|:------|:----|:----|:------------|:---------|
| 1 | A101 – Site Clearance | Bulldozer D8 | 200 kW | 1 | 120 | 01-Jan | 10-Jan | 80 | 2,500 | 2,00,000 |
| 2 | A102 – Excavation | Excavator CAT 320 | 1.5 m³ | 1 | 191.25 | 11-Jan | 30-Apr | 800 | 2,000 | 16,00,000 |
| 3 | A102 – Hauling | Dumper 6 m³ | 6 m³ | 24 | 56.8 | 11-Jan | 30-Apr | 800 | 1,500 | 2,88,00,000 |
| 4 | A103 – Compaction | Vibratory Roller | 12 t | 2 | 90 | 15-Jan | 30-Apr | 720 | 1,800 | 25,92,000 |
| **Σ** | | | | | | | | | | **₹ 3,31,92,000** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Equipment Schedule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Inputs] --> B1[Work Quantities BOQ]
    A0 --> B2[Activity Network CPM]
    A0 --> B3[Site Conditions Soil Report]
    A0 --> B4[Time Schedule Bar Chart]

    B1 --> C1[Equipment Selection Engine]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1

    C1 --> D1[Productivity Estimation Cycle Time Formula]
    C1 --> D2[Fleet Size Calculation Balanced Fleet Eqn]
    C1 --> D3[Cost Estimation Owning plus Operating]

    D1 --> E1[Equipment Schedule Master Table]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[Procurement Plan Own Rent Lease]
    E1 --> F2[Daily Deployment Chart]
    E1 --> F3[Cost Control Baseline]
    E1 --> F4[Cash Flow Forecast]

    F1 --> G0[Project Execution]
    F2 --> G0
    F3 --> G0
    F4 --> G0
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of Equipment Schedule Preparation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step1 Quantify Work] --> stepB[Step2 List Activities]
    stepB --> stepC[Step3 Match Activity to Machine Type]
    stepC --> stepD[Step4 Calculate Productivity]
    stepD --> stepE[Step5 Compute Fleet Size]
    stepE --> stepF[Step6 Compute Hourly Cost]
    stepF --> stepG[Step7 Allocate Time Window]
    stepG --> stepH[Step8 Compile Master Table]
    stepH --> stepI[Step9 Review and Approve]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step10 Issue to Site]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic – Own vs. Rent vs. Lease

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{Duration of Need} --> lt3[Less than 3 months]
    start --> mid3[3 to 12 months]
    start --> gt12[More than 12 months]

    lt3 --> rent1[Recommendation RENT Hired]
    mid3 --> lease1[Recommendation LEASE]
    gt12 --> own1[Recommendation OWN Purchase]

    rent1 --> cost1[Pay only usage hours]
    lease1 --> cost2[Fixed monthly + maintenance]
    own1 --> cost3[Depreciation + full control]
```

## 4.4 Equipment Schedule Hierarchy (Indentation View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Project Equipment Schedule]

    P0 --> Q1[Earthworks Division]
    P0 --> Q2[Structural Division]
    P0 --> Q3[MEP Division]

    Q1 --> R1[Excavation Fleet]
    Q1 --> R2[Hauling Fleet]
    Q1 --> R3[Compaction Fleet]

    R1 --> S1[Excavators]
    R1 --> S2[Loaders]
    R2 --> S3[Dumpers]
    R2 --> S4[Tipper Trucks]
    R3 --> S5[Rollers]
    R3 --> S6[Compactors]
```
