# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A – 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Equipment Schedule" as used in Construction Project Management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An Equipment Schedule is a time-phased, tabular planning document that lists all construction machinery, equipment, and plant required for a project along with their **type, capacity, quantity, deployment window, productivity, hourly cost, and total project cost**. It integrates the project work breakdown structure with the construction schedule (CPM) and the cost estimate, and acts as the primary control instrument for plant management, procurement, and cash-flow forecasting. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any SIX components/columns of a standard Equipment Schedule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The six essential columns of an Equipment Schedule are:
1. Equipment description and specification
2. Quantity required
3. Productivity / output
4. Deployment timing (start–end)
5. Duration of use (hours)
6. Hourly rate (owning + operating) and total cost *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B – 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
A construction project involves excavation of **80,000 m³** of ordinary earth (bank measure). The haul distance is **2 km**. The contractor proposes to use:
- One **hydraulic excavator** with bucket capacity $q = 1.2$ m³, ideal cycle time = **0.30 min**, efficiency = 0.80
- **Dumpers** with struck capacity = **8 m³**, fixed time = **1.2 min**, speed loaded = **18 km/h**, speed empty = **28 km/h**, efficiency = 0.85
- Working hours = 8 hr/day, **6 days/week**, target completion = **5 months**

Determine:
**(a)** The balanced number of dumpers per excavator. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The total project equipment cost if excavator hire = ₹1,800/hr and dumper hire = ₹1,200/hr. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) – 7 marks

**Step 1 – Compute dumper cycle time:**

$$
T_H = 1.2 + \frac{2 \times 60}{18} + \frac{2 \times 60}{28}
$$

(2 km at 18 km/h = 2/18 h = 6.67 min; 2 km at 28 km/h = 2/28 h = 4.29 min)

$$
T_H = 1.2 + 6.67 + 4.29 = 12.16 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 – Apply the balanced fleet formula:**

$$
n = \frac{12.16}{0.30} + 1 = 40.53 + 1 = 41.53 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{n = 42 \text{ dumpers}}
$$

#### Model Solution (b) – 7 marks

**Step 1 – Available hours (5 months ≈ 22 weeks × 6 days = 132 days):**

$$
H_{avail} = 132 \times 8 = 1056 \text{ hr}
$$

**Step 2 – Excavator productivity:**

$$
Q_L = \frac{60 \times 1.2 \times 0.80}{0.30} = 192 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 3 – Number of excavators required:**

$$
N_L = \frac{80{,}000}{192 \times 1056} = \frac{80{,}000}{2{,}02{,}752} = 0.39 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{N_L = 1}
$$

**Step 4 – Total cost:**

$$
C_T = (1 \times 1056 \times 1800) + (42 \times 1056 \times 1200)
$$

$$
C_T = 19{,}00{,}800 + 5{,}32{,}22{,}400 = \boxed{₹\,5{,}51{,}23{,}200}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **components/columns of a typical Equipment Schedule** used in a building project. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A **tower crane** is purchased for ₹**80,00,000** with a salvage value of ₹**8,00,000**. Its economic life is **8 years**, working **2000 hr/year**. The average interest rate is **12%**. Annual insurance is ₹60,000, taxes ₹40,000, storage ₹20,000. Calculate the **owning cost per hour**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) – 7 marks
A well-labelled Equipment Schedule should contain these columns (table format expected):

| Item | Activity | Equipment | Spec | Qty | Output | Start | End | Hours | Rate (₹/hr) | Total ₹ | Source |
|:-----|:---------|:----------|:-----|:----|:-------|:------|:----|:------|:------------|:--------|:-------|

Each row corresponds to **one type of equipment for one activity**. The schedule is then *time-synchronized* with the project bar chart and *cost-summarized* for the BOQ. *(7 marks – 4 for list, 3 for explanation of how it is integrated with schedule & cost)*

#### Model Solution (b) – 7 marks

**Step 1 – Depreciation per hour:**

$$
D = \frac{P - S}{H \times L} = \frac{80{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{2000 \times 8} = \frac{72{,}00{,}000}{16{,}000} = ₹\,450/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 2 – Interest per hour (on average investment):**

$$
I_{hr} = \frac{(P+S)}{2} \times \frac{i}{H} = \frac{80{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000}{2} \times \frac{0.12}{2000}
$$

$$
I_{hr} = 44{,}00{,}000 \times 6 \times 10^{-5} = ₹\,264/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 3 – Insurance, Tax, Storage per hour:**

$$
ITS = \frac{60{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 20{,}000}{2000} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{2000} = ₹\,60/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 4 – Total Owning Cost per hour:**

$$
\boxed{C_o = 450 + 264 + 60 = ₹\,774/\text{hr}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1"** in the balanced fleet equation – **2 marks penalty** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Unit-mismatch error**: forgetting to convert km to minutes (multiply by 60) in haul-speed calculations.
> 3. **Bank vs. Loose confusion**: forgetting the swell factor when computing hauler productivity.
> 4. **Rounding off too early**: Round only at the *final* fleet size; intermediate values must be in decimals.
> 5. **Owning cost**: Many students write the *depreciation only* and miss interest/insurance/tax/storage. The Peurifoy model has **four** components – do not skip any.
> 6. **Idle/Standby cost** is not the same as *owning cost*. Examiners expect students to *state the assumption* that all listed hours are working hours.
> 7. **Rental vs. Owned confusion**: In a rented-equipment problem, owning cost = 0; only operating cost (hire + fuel + operator) applies.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 ESE – Equipment Schedule)**

- ✅ Equipment Schedule = **time-phased plant register** linking work quantities, schedule, and cost.
- ✅ It is built **after** WBS, **along with** CPM, and **before** final BOQ.
- ✅ The **balanced fleet formula** $n = T_H/T_L + 1$ is the single most-tested equation.
- ✅ Convert **all speeds to minutes** before substitution: $T = L \times 60 / V$.
- ✅ Use **swell factor (1.20–1.35)** to convert bank → loose volume for hauler sizing.
- ✅ Productivity formula: $Q = 60 \times q \times E / T_c$ (bank m³/hr).
- ✅ Owning cost has **4 parts**: Depreciation, Interest, Insurance/Tax, Storage.
- ✅ Operating cost has **5 parts**: Fuel, Lubricant, Maintenance, Operator, Wear (tires/tracks).
- ✅ Always **round up** the number of machines to the next whole integer.
- ✅ Fleet cost in project = **Qty × Hours × Hourly Rate**, summed over all rows.
- ✅ A blank **Source** column (Own/Rent/Lease) is good practice and fetches bonus marks.
- ✅ Match the equipment type to the activity: do not use a *bulldozer* where an *excavator* is required, and vice versa.
- ✅ Cross-check that the **total project hours × productivity ≥ total quantity** (sanity check).
- ✅ The Equipment Schedule must be **revised every month** based on actual progress – it is a *living document*, not a one-time artifact.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financial Schedule. Techniques of Planning - Bar Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET604 - CONSTRUCTION PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Financial Schedule. Techniques of Planning - Bar Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Financial Schedule — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Financial Schedule (KTU Definition):** A *Financial Schedule* (also termed a **Cost Schedule** or **Expenditure Schedule**) is a time-phased projection of the monetary resources required to execute a construction project. It correlates the **time dimension** of the construction programme with the **cost dimension**, producing a planned cash outflow profile — typically represented as an **S-Curve** (also called the *Expenditure Curve* or *Cost-Time Curve*).

The financial schedule is derived by superimposing the **Time Schedule** (Bar Chart / Network) onto the **Cost Estimate** (BOQ + Rate Analysis). The output is a month-wise or week-wise statement of expected expenditure across the project lifecycle — from mobilization to demobilization.

### Components of a Financial Schedule

| # | Component | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Time Axis (X-axis)** | Project duration in weeks/months |
| 2 | **Cumulative Cost Axis (Y-axis)** | Money spent till date (in ₹ Lakhs / Crores) |
| 3 | **Planned Expenditure Curve** | The intended S-Curve |
| 4 | **Actual Expenditure Curve** | Real money spent, plotted alongside |
| 5 | **Earned Value (BCWS)** | Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled |

## 1.2 Bar Chart — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bar Chart (Gantt Chart):** A **Bar Chart** is a graphical representation of a construction schedule in which activities are listed on the **vertical axis** and **time** (days/weeks/months) is plotted on the **horizontal axis**. Each activity is represented by a **horizontal bar** whose **length is proportional to the duration** of that activity, and whose **position** indicates its start and finish times.

The Bar Chart was popularized by **Henry L. Gantt** (1910s) and remains the most widely used planning tool on small-to-medium construction sites.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Wedding Planner" 🎂**
>
> Imagine planning a wedding in **6 months**:
> - **Venue booking** (Day 1 – Day 30) → First horizontal bar
> - **Catering confirmation** (Day 20 – Day 60) → Second bar (overlapping the first)
> - **Invitation printing** (Day 50 – Day 80) → Third bar
>
> The **Bar Chart** is simply a **visual calendar** of these tasks. Now imagine you also write the **cost of each task** above its bar (₹50,000 for venue, ₹2,00,000 for catering…) and add them up *month by month*. That running total, plotted on a graph, gives you the **Financial Schedule (S-Curve)**.
>
> In short: **Bar Chart = What happens & when? | Financial Schedule = How much money & when?**

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Project Currency Unit:** ₹ Lakhs (for Indian KTU projects) or **$ Million** (international).
- **S-Curve Standard Shape:** Initial slow rise (mobilization) → steep middle slope (peak construction) → tapering tail (finishing, snagging).
- **Bar Chart Standard Time Granularity:** **1 day = 1 mm to 5 mm** of bar length (IS 9834 conventions).
- **Typical Bar Chart Resolution:** Weekly for projects < 6 months, Monthly for projects > 1 year.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is a Financial Schedule Needed?

A financial schedule is essential because **money is a constrained resource** — contractors need to know:

1. **Cash flow requirements** — How much working capital must be arranged?
2. **Bank loan scheduling** — When to draw down and when to repay?
3. **Progress billing** — When to raise invoices to the client?
4. **Cost control** — Comparing *planned* vs *actual* expenditure.
5. **Liquidity management** — Avoiding insolvency mid-project.

## 2.2 Construction Logic of an S-Curve

The S-Curve is **S-shaped** because of three distinct phases of a project:

### Phase 1 — Slow Start (Ramp-up)
- Mobilization of plant, labour, site offices.
- Few activities running → low expenditure.
- Duration: typically **5%–15%** of total project time.

### Phase 2 — Steep Middle (Peak Construction)
- Maximum manpower deployed.
- Bulk materials (cement, steel, aggregate) consumed.
- Many parallel activities → high, near-linear expenditure.
- Duration: typically **60%–75%** of total project time.

### Phase 3 — Tapering Tail (Finishing)
- Finishing works, testing, commissioning, demobilization.
- Reduced workforce.
- Duration: typically **10%–20%** of total project time.

## 2.3 Derivation of the S-Curve Equation

The cumulative expenditure $C(t)$ at time $t$ is often modeled as a **logistic (sigmoid) function**:

$$
C(t) = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{total}}$ = Total project cost (₹ Lakhs)
- $t_0$ = Inflection point (time of maximum rate of expenditure)
- $k$ = Steepness constant (controls slope of middle phase)
- $e$ = Euler's number $\approx 2.71828$

A simpler polynomial approximation is also used:

$$
C(t) = a t^2 + b t
$$

Where $a$ and $b$ are determined from boundary conditions at $t=0$ and $t=T$.

## 2.4 Bar Chart — Operational Logic

A bar chart is constructed using the following **step-by-step procedure**:

1. **List all activities** from the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS).
2. **Estimate duration** of each activity (using IS 7272 norms or work-study data).
3. **Identify dependencies** — which activity must precede which?
4. **Sequence activities** — earliest start (ES) and earliest finish (EF).
5. **Draw the time scale** on the horizontal axis.
6. **Plot horizontal bars** for each activity, starting at its start date, with length = duration.
7. **Mark milestones** as triangular or diamond symbols.
8. **Add float/slack** as a dashed line after the solid bar (for free float).
9. **Overlay a progress line** (vertical line indicating "today").

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every formula you may need for Module 1 problems on Bar Charts and Financial Schedules. Master these for KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Application |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $C(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \cdot P_i(t)$ | $c_i$ = cost of activity $i$; $P_i(t)$ = % progress of activity $i$ at time $t$ | Cumulative cost at any time |
| 2 | $S = \frac{C(t)}{C_{\text{total}}}$ | $S$ = S-Curve ordinate (0 to 1) | Normalized S-Curve |
| 3 | $\text{BCWS} = \text{PV} = \sum (\text{Planned cost to date})$ | Earned Value term | Schedule baseline |
| 4 | $\text{BCWP} = \text{EV} = \sum (\text{% complete} \times \text{budget})$ | Earned Value | Work performed value |
| 5 | $\text{ACWP} = \text{AC} = \sum (\text{actual cost to date})$ | Earned Value | Money spent |
| 6 | $\text{SV} = \text{BCWP} - \text{BCWS}$ | Schedule Variance | Positive = ahead |
| 7 | $\text{CV} = \text{BCWP} - \text{ACWP}$ | Cost Variance | Positive = under budget |
| 8 | $\text{SPI} = \text{BCWP} / \text{BCWS}$ | Schedule Performance Index | $> 1$ = ahead |
| 9 | $\text{CPI} = \text{BCWP} / \text{ACWP}$ | Cost Performance Index | $> 1$ = efficient |
| 10 | $T_{\text{bar}} = \frac{\text{Duration of activity}}{1 \text{ day}}$ (in mm) | Drawing scale | Bar length on chart |
| 11 | $\text{% Progress} = \frac{\text{Bar completed}}{\text{Total bar length}} \times 100$ | Monitoring | Site reporting |
| 12 | $C_{\text{month}} = C_{\text{total}} \times f_{\text{month}}$ | Where $f_{\text{month}}$ is the phasing factor | Monthly billing |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **BCWS** (Planned Value) with **BCWP** (Earned Value). Remember: **BCWS = "What you PLANNED to spend"**; **BCWP = "What the WORK is actually WORTH"**. The S-Curve plots **BCWS** against time.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Contractor cash-flow planning:** Banks (SBI, ICICI) disburse loan amounts phased as per the S-Curve.
- **Client milestone payments:** Many FIDIC contracts use S-Curve percentages to release advances.
- **Government project monitoring:** PM Gati Shakti & CPWD use S-Curves for project dashboards.
- **Dispute resolution:** Varied S-Curve (planned vs actual) is evidence in arbitration.
- **Resource levelling:** Bars indicate peak manpower periods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — S-Curve from a Bar Chart

**Problem Statement:**
A residential project has the following data. Construct the **monthly Financial Schedule (S-Curve)**.

| Activity | Duration (months) | Start Month | Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|----------------|
| A — Excavation | 2 | Month 1 | 20 |
| B — Foundation | 3 | Month 2 | 45 |
| C — Superstructure | 5 | Month 4 | 120 |
| D — Brickwork | 4 | Month 7 | 60 |
| E — Finishing | 3 | Month 10 | 35 |
| F — Services & Handover | 2 | Month 12 | 20 |

Total Duration = **13 months** | Total Cost = **₹ 300 Lakhs**

### Step 1 — Allocate Monthly Cost (assuming uniform distribution within each activity)

| Month | Activities Running | Monthly Cost (₹ L) | Cumulative Cost (₹ L) |
|:-----:|:-------------------|:-------------------:|:---------------------:|
| 1 | A | $20/2 = 10$ | 10 |
| 2 | A, B | $10 + 45/3 = 10 + 15 = 25$ | 35 |
| 3 | B | $15$ | 50 |
| 4 | B, C | $15 + 120/5 = 15 + 24 = 39$ | 89 |
| 5 | C | $24$ | 113 |
| 6 | C | $24$ | 137 |
| 7 | C, D | $24 + 60/4 = 24 + 15 = 39$ | 176 |
| 8 | C, D | $24 + 15 = 39$ | 215 |
| 9 | D | $15$ | 230 |
| 10 | D, E | $15 + 35/3 \approx 15 + 11.67 = 26.67$ | 256.67 |
| 11 | E | $11.67$ | 268.34 |
| 12 | E, F | $11.67 + 20/2 = 11.67 + 10 = 21.67$ | 290.01 |
| 13 | F | $10$ | 300.01 ≈ 300 |

### Step 2 — Calculate S-Curve Normalized Coordinates

$$
S(t) = \frac{C(t)}{C_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\text{Cumulative Cost at month } t}{300}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(1) &= \frac{10}{300} = 0.0333 \\
S(2) &= \frac{35}{300} = 0.1167 \\
S(3) &= \frac{50}{300} = 0.1667 \\
S(4) &= \frac{89}{300} = 0.2967 \\
S(5) &= \frac{113}{300} = 0.3767 \\
S(6) &= \frac{137}{300} = 0.4567 \\
S(7) &= \frac{176}{300} = 0.5867 \\
S(8) &= \frac{215}{300} = 0.7167 \\
S(9) &= \frac{230}{300} = 0.7667 \\
S(10) &= \frac{256.67}{300} = 0.8556 \\
S(11) &= \frac{268.34}{300} = 0.8945 \\
S(12) &= \frac{290.01}{300} = 0.9667 \\
S(13) &= \frac{300}{300} = 1.0000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify the S-Curve is "S-Shaped"

Checking the differences $\Delta S$ month-on-month:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_1 &= 0.0333 \\
\Delta S_2 &= 0.0834 \\
\Delta S_3 &= 0.0500 \\
\Delta S_4 &= 0.1300 \\
\Delta S_5 &= 0.0800 \\
\Delta S_6 &= 0.0800 \\
\Delta S_7 &= 0.1300 \\
\Delta S_8 &= 0.1300 \\
\Delta S_9 &= 0.0500 \\
\Delta S_{10} &= 0.0889 \\
\Delta S_{11} &= 0.0389 \\
\Delta S_{12} &= 0.0722 \\
\Delta S_{13} &= 0.0333 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> The differences **rise → plateau → fall** — confirming the characteristic **S-shape**.

### Step 4 — Earned Value Calculations (KTU High-Yield)

Suppose at the **end of Month 6**, the site engineer reports:
- Excavation: 100% complete
- Foundation: 100% complete
- Superstructure: 50% complete
- Others: 0%

**BCWS** (Planned Value at Month 6) = Cumulative planned cost = **₹ 137 L**

**BCWP** (Earned Value) = Sum of (Budget × % Complete) for each activity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BCWP} &= (20 \times 1.00) + (45 \times 1.00) + (120 \times 0.50) + 0 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 20 + 45 + 60 = 125 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**ACWP** (Actual Cost) — say contractor reports **₹ 145 Lakhs** spent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SV} &= \text{BCWP} - \text{BCWS} = 125 - 137 = -12 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{CV} &= \text{BCWP} - \text{ACWP} = 125 - 145 = -20 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{SPI} &= \frac{125}{137} = 0.912 \\
\text{CPI} &= \frac{125}{145} = 0.862
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\text{SV} = -12$ → Project is **behind schedule** by ₹ 12 Lakhs worth of work.
- $\text{CV} = -20$ → Project is **over budget** by ₹ 20 Lakhs.
- $\text{SPI} < 1$ and $\text{CPI} < 1$ → **Recovery plan required.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Bar Chart vs Linked Bar Chart

| Feature | Simple Bar Chart | Linked Bar Chart (Precedence Diagram) |
|---------|------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Dependency shown?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (by linking arrows/lines) |
| **Float shown?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (dashed lines) |
| **Critical path?** | ❌ Cannot identify | ✅ Can be identified |
| **Complexity** | Low | Moderate |
| **Use** | Small projects, site display | Medium projects |
| **Tool** | Manual / Excel | MS Project / Primavera |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Construction of a Bar Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Receive WBS and BOQ"] --> B["List All Activities"]
    B --> C["Estimate Duration of Each Activity"]
    C --> D["Identify Dependencies and Predecessors"]
    D --> E["Sequence Activities: ES and EF"]
    E --> F["Draw Horizontal Time Scale"]
    F --> G["Plot Horizontal Bars for Each Activity"]
    G --> H["Add Milestones as Triangular Markers"]
    H --> I["Mark Float with Dashed Lines"]
    I --> J["Overlay Progress Line for Monitoring"]
    J --> K["Final Bar Chart Published"]
    K --> L["Use for Daily Site Coordination"]
    K --> M["Convert to S-Curve by Adding Cost Data"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Derivation of Financial Schedule (S-Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Time Schedule (Bar Chart)"] --> C["Combine Activity + Cost Data"]
    B["Cost Estimate (BOQ)"] --> C
    C --> D["Compute Monthly Expenditure"]
    D --> E["Compute Cumulative Expenditure C of t"]
    E --> F["Normalize: S of t equals C of t divided by C total"]
    F --> G["Plot S vs Time: S-Curve"]
    G --> H["Compare Planned vs Actual S-Curve"]
    H --> I{"Variance Detected?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Apply Corrective Action"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Continue Monitoring"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Components of a Bar Chart

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 2
    block:axis["Bar Chart Layout"]:2
        Y["Vertical Axis: Activity Names (WBS)"]
        X["Horizontal Axis: Time Scale (Days/Weeks/Months)"]
    end
    block:visual["Visual Elements"]:2
        bar1["Solid Bar: Actual Duration"]
        bar2["Dashed Bar: Float / Slack"]
    end
    block:mark["Special Markers"]:2
        m1["Triangle: Milestone"]
        m2["Diamond: Key Deliverable"]
    end
    block:monitor["Monitoring Layer"]:2
        p1["Vertical Progress Line: Today"]
        p2["Shaded Region: Work Completed"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flow — Earned Value Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["BCWS Planned Value"] --> S["SV = BCWP minus BCWS"]
    E["BCWP Earned Value"] --> S
    E --> V["CV = BCWP minus ACWP"]
    A["ACWP Actual Cost"] --> V
    S --> SI["SPI = BCWP divided by BCWS"]
    V --> CI["CPI = BCWP divided by ACWP"]
    SI --> R{"SPI less than 1?"}
    CI --> R2{"CPI less than 1?"}
    R -- "Yes" --> W1["Behind Schedule - Crash or Fast-Track"]
    R2 -- "Yes" --> W2["Over Budget - Cost Control"]
    R -- "No" --> OK1["On Schedule"]
    R2 -- "No" --> OK2["Within Budget"]
```

## 4.5 Visual Schema of an S-Curve (Text Representation)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Project S-Curve (Cumulative Cost vs Time)"
    x-axis ["M1", "M2", "M3", "M4", "M5", "M6", "M7", "M8", "M9", "M10", "M11", "M12", "M13"]
    y-axis "Cumulative Cost (₹ Lakhs)" 0 --> 320
    line [10, 35, 50, 89, 113, 137, 176, 215, 230, 256.67, 268.34, 290, 300]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the S-Curve above:** The slope between any two months gives the **monthly burn rate**. The steepest slope is between **Month 4 and Month 8** — this is the **peak construction phase** when the contractor needs maximum cash.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

### Q1. Define a Bar Chart. List any four advantages of a Bar Chart in construction project management.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Bar Chart is a graphical representation of a project schedule in which activities are listed on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis, with each activity shown as a horizontal bar whose length is proportional to its duration.

**Advantages:**
1. **Simple to understand** — even non-technical clients can read it.
2. **Easy to prepare** — requires only activity list, durations, and a ruler.
3. **Visual progress tracking** — a vertical "today" line shows lag/lead.
4. **Effective for site coordination** — supervisors can sequence daily work.
*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for any two advantages, 1 mark for the remaining two)*

---

### Q2. What is a Financial Schedule? Why is it important in construction projects?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Financial Schedule is a time-phased statement of project expenditure, typically represented as an **S-Curve**, showing how project cost accumulates over the project duration.

**Importance:**
1. **Cash flow planning** — contractors arrange working capital based on this.
2. **Bank loan drawdown** — scheduled in line with the S-Curve.
3. **Progress billing to client** — milestone payments tied to S-Curve percentages.
4. **Cost control** — planned vs actual S-Curve comparison reveals overruns early.
*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any four importance points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 1 Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Bar Chart + Linked Bar Chart

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction procedure of a Bar Chart** for a small building project. List **six advantages** and **four limitations** of a Bar Chart. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between a **simple Bar Chart** and a **Linked Bar Chart**. Illustrate the **conversion of a simple Bar Chart into a Linked Bar Chart** with an example of at least **six activities**, showing **precedence relationships, free float, and total float**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Divide the project into a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) — list all activities.

| Activity | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| A | Site clearance & excavation |
| B | PCC for foundation |
| C | RCC footing & column up to plinth |
| D | Backfilling & plinth beam |
| E | Superstructure brickwork |
| F | Lintels, chajja, slab casting |
| G | Plastering & finishing |

**Step 2:** Estimate duration of each activity using IS 7272 norms.

**Step 3:** Determine start/finish dates and dependencies.

**Step 4:** Draw the horizontal time scale (e.g., 1 week = 1 cm).

**Step 5:** Plot each activity as a horizontal bar starting at its start date.

**Step 6:** Mark milestones with triangular symbols.

**Step 7:** Overlay a vertical "progress line" updated weekly.

**Advantages of Bar Chart (any 6, ½ mark each = 3 marks):**
1. Simple, visual, and easy to interpret.
2. Requires minimal training to prepare.
3. Suitable for repetitive projects (e.g., housing colonies).
4. Effective for daily/weekly site meetings.
5. Cost of preparation is very low.
6. Can be drawn by hand on a chart paper.
7. Useful for client communication.

**Limitations of Bar Chart (any 4, ½ mark each = 2 marks):**
1. Does **not show inter-activity dependencies** clearly.
2. **Float/slack cannot be visualized**.
3. **Critical activities cannot be identified** without manual analysis.
4. Becomes **cluttered for large projects** (>100 activities).
5. **Does not show resource requirements**.
6. **No indication of cost**.

**Part (b) — Linked Bar Chart (7 marks):**

**Difference Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Simple Bar Chart | Linked Bar Chart |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Dependency | Not shown | Shown by linking lines |
| Float | Not shown | Shown as dashed extension |
| Critical Path | Not identifiable | Identifiable |
| Complexity | Low | Moderate |
| Use | Small projects | Medium projects |

**Conversion Example (4 marks):**

Consider 6 activities A, B, C, D, E, F with the following data:

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) | ES | EF | LS | LF | Total Float |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|----|----|----|----|-------------|
| A | — | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 (CP) |
| B | A | 6 | 4 | 10 | 4 | 10 | 0 (CP) |
| C | A | 5 | 4 | 9 | 8 | 13 | 4 |
| D | B | 7 | 10 | 17 | 10 | 17 | 0 (CP) |
| E | C | 4 | 9 | 13 | 13 | 17 | 4 |
| F | D, E | 3 | 17 | 20 | 17 | 20 | 0 (CP) |

- **Critical Path:** A → B → D → F (Total duration = 20 days).
- **Free Float of C** = ES of next activity (E) − EF of C = 9 − 9 = 0.
- **Total Float of C** = LF − EF = 13 − 9 = 4 days.
- **Float of E** = LF − EF = 17 − 13 = 4 days.

In the linked bar chart, the **solid portion** of each bar shows the activity duration and the **dashed portion** shows the float. **Arrows** link each activity to its successor.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Financial Schedule (S-Curve)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Alternative]**

**(a)** Define a **Financial Schedule**. Sketch the **typical S-Curve** of a construction project and explain the **three phases** it represents. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A construction project has the following data. Prepare a **monthly financial schedule** and plot the **S-Curve**. Compute the **earned value parameters (BCWS, BCWP, SV, CV, SPI, CPI)** at the end of month 7, given that activities C and D are 40% and 20% complete respectively, and the contractor has spent ₹ 90 Lakhs till then. **(7 marks)**

| Activity | Duration (months) | Start Month | Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|----------------|
| A | 1 | 1 | 10 |
| B | 2 | 2 | 30 |
| C | 3 | 3 | 60 |
| D | 3 | 5 | 50 |
| E | 2 | 7 | 30 |

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Definition + S-Curve Phases (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A Financial Schedule is a time-phased projection of expected expenditure across the project duration, usually represented as an S-shaped curve (S-Curve) plotting cumulative cost against time.

**Typical S-Curve Sketch (described, 2 marks):**

```
Cost (₹ L)
  |
  |              _____________________
  |           /
  |          /
  |         /  <- Peak construction
  |        /
  |       /
  |      /
  |     /
  |____/_____________________________ Time
  M0   M3   M6   M9   M12
  Phase1 Phase2  Phase3
```

**Three Phases (3 marks):**

1. **Phase I — Ramp-up (Slow Start):** Mobilization of men, machinery, materials. Few activities, low expenditure. Lasts 5%–15% of project time.
2. **Phase II — Peak Construction (Steep Slope):** Maximum concurrent activities, bulk material consumption. Linear-to-steep rise. Lasts 60%–75% of project time.
3. **Phase III — Tapering Tail (Finishing):** Finishing, testing, demobilization. Decreasing workforce, flattening curve. Lasts 10%–20% of project time.

**Part (b) — Monthly Financial Schedule + Earned Value (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Monthly Cost Distribution (3 marks):**

| Month | Active Activities | Monthly Cost (₹ L) | Cumulative (₹ L) |
|:-----:|:------------------|:-------------------:|:-----------------:|
| 1 | A | $10/1 = 10$ | 10 |
| 2 | A, B | $10 + 30/2 = 10 + 15 = 25$ | 35 |
| 3 | B, C | $15 + 60/3 = 15 + 20 = 35$ | 70 |
| 4 | C | $20$ | 90 |
| 5 | C, D | $20 + 50/3 \approx 20 + 16.67 = 36.67$ | 126.67 |
| 6 | D | $16.67$ | 143.34 |
| 7 | D, E | $16.67 + 30/2 = 16.67 + 15 = 31.67$ | 175.01 |
| 8 | E | $15$ | 190.01 |

Total = **₹ 190 Lakhs** over **8 months**.

**Step 2 — S-Curve Coordinates (1 mark):** Normalize: $S(t) = C(t)/190$.

**Step 3 — Earned Value at End of Month 7 (3 marks):**

- **BCWS** (Planned cumulative cost) at Month 7 = **₹ 175 L**.
- **BCWP** (Earned Value) = (Cost of completed work):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BCWP} &= (A: 10 \times 1.0) + (B: 30 \times 1.0) + (C: 60 \times 0.40) + (D: 50 \times 0.20) + (E: 30 \times 0) \\
&= 10 + 30 + 24 + 10 + 0 = 74 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **ACWP** = ₹ 90 Lakhs (given).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SV} &= \text{BCWP} - \text{BCWS} = 74 - 175 = -101 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{CV} &= \text{BCWP} - \text{ACWP} = 74 - 90 = -16 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{SPI} &= \frac{74}{175} = 0.423 \\
\text{CPI} &= \frac{74}{90} = 0.822
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{SPI} = 0.42$ indicates the project is **significantly behind schedule**, and $\text{CPI} = 0.82$ indicates **cost overrun**. The contractor must **fast-track remaining activities** and implement **strict cost-control measures**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SV/CV signs:** Many students compute SV = BCWS − BCWP (reverse sign). The correct formula is **SV = BCWP − BCWS**. A *negative* SV means **behind schedule** — counter-intuitive but standard.
> 2. **S-Curve drawing:** The S-Curve must be plotted with **cumulative cost on Y-axis and time on X-axis**, with both axes labelled with units. Students often forget the S-shape justification.
> 3. **Float in Linked Bar Charts:** Free Float ≠ Total Float. **FF = ES(successor) − EF(current)**, while **TF = LF − EF**. Many students use the same formula for both.
> 4. **Monthly distribution:** Forgetting to divide activity cost by duration (assuming total cost in one month) — this is a **fatal error** in S-Curve problems.
> 5. **Critical Path:** In Q1(b), the critical path must be traced as a **sequence of activities with zero total float**, not merely "the longest path" (which is coincidentally true but not the definition).

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**

- ✅ **Bar Chart** = Activities on Y-axis, Time on X-axis, horizontal bars proportional to duration.
- ✅ **Gantt** introduced it in 1910s; still the **most widely used** simple planning tool.
- ✅ **Advantages of Bar Chart:** Simple, visual, easy progress tracking, low cost.
- ✅ **Limitations of Bar Chart:** No dependencies, no float, no critical path, no cost data, cluttered for large projects.
- ✅ **Linked Bar Chart** overcomes dependency & float limitations using arrows and dashed extensions.
- ✅ **Critical Path** = sequence of activities with **zero total float**; it is the **longest path** through the network.
- ✅ **Free Float (FF)** = $\text{ES of successor} - \text{EF of current activity}$.
- ✅ **Total Float (TF)** = $\text{LF} - \text{EF} = \text{LS} - \text{ES}$.
- ✅ **Financial Schedule (S-Curve)** = time-phased cumulative cost plot, S-shaped.
- ✅ **S-Curve Phases:** Slow start (mobilization) → Steep middle (peak construction) → Tapering tail (finishing).
- ✅ **S-Curve equation:** $C(t) = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{1 + e^{-k(t-t_0)}}$ (logistic model).
- ✅ **BCWS** (Planned Value) → baseline of S-Curve.
- ✅ **BCWP** (Earned Value) = $\sum (\% \text{ complete} \times \text{budget})$.
- ✅ **ACWP** (Actual Cost) = real money spent.
- ✅ **SV = BCWP − BCWS** (negative = behind schedule).
- ✅ **CV = BCWP − ACWP** (negative = over budget).
- ✅ **SPI = BCWP / BCWS** (>1 = ahead, <1 = behind).
- ✅ **CPI = BCWP / ACWP** (>1 = efficient, <1 = inefficient).
- ✅ **KTU Hot Tip:** In ESE, always draw the S-Curve **smoothly** (not as discrete dots) and label all axes. The examiner awards 1 full mark just for a **well-labelled, neat sketch**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mile Stone Charts. Network Representation - Activity on Arrow (AoA) or Activity on Node (AoN) Diagram. Network Analysis - Critical Path Method (CPM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET604 - CONSTRUCTION PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mile Stone Charts. Network Representation - Activity on Arrow (AoA) or Activity on Node (AoN) Diagram. Network Analysis - Critical Path Method (CPM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Milestone Charts

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> A **Milestone Chart** is a specialized bar-chart-based project management tool that represents significant, identifiable events (milestones) in a project schedule rather than the continuous duration of activities. Each milestone marks the completion of a major deliverable, phase, or check-point, and is represented by a distinct symbol (typically a diamond, circle, or triangle) plotted against a calibrated time axis.

A milestone in a construction project is a **zero-duration event** — it consumes no time or resources, but it is a critical checkpoint. Examples in a building project include: "Foundation Laid," "Roof Slab Cast," "Plumbing Completed," "Building Handed Over."

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**

Imagine a railway journey from Thiruvananthapuram to Kasaragod. Instead of showing the train's speed at every kilometer, a milestone chart would mark the stations where the train **must arrive by a certain time**. Trivandrum Central → Kollam → Kottayam → Ernakulam → Thrissur → Kozhikode → Kasaragod. Each station is a **milestone** (a target event), and the date stamped on the chart is the target arrival time. If the train crosses Trivandrum on Day 1 and Kollam on Day 2, the *spacing* on the chart visually shows the planned **duration between milestones**. This is exactly how a Milestone Chart works in a construction project.

**Key Standard Metrics & Symbols:**

- **Symbol for Milestone:** Diamond (♦), Circle (○), or Triangle (▲)
- **Y-axis:** Lists the project events/phases
- **X-axis:** Calibrated time scale (days/weeks/months)
- **Critical metric:** **Target Date** for each milestone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Milestone charts are classified as a **primitive scheduling tool** — they are *not* a true network analysis method. They show **what** must be achieved and **when**, but **do not show logical interdependencies** between activities. This is precisely why network methods (CPM/PERT) were developed as successors.

---

### 1.2 Network Representation – Activity on Arrow (AoA) and Activity on Node (AoN)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> A **Project Network** is a logical, diagrammatic representation of the sequential and parallel relationships among all activities required to complete a project. It is the foundational tool of modern project management and forms the basis for the **Critical Path Method (CPM)**.

There are two principal conventions for drawing project networks:

#### 1.2.1 Activity on Arrow (AoA) — also called Arrow Diagramming Method (ADM)

- **Activities** are represented as **directed arrows (→)**.
- **Events / Nodes** (start or finish of activities) are represented as **circles (O)** or **nodes**.
- The **head** of an arrow = completion event; the **tail** = start event.
- A dummy activity (dashed arrow, **→ --- →**) is needed to show logical dependencies that cannot be expressed by real activities alone.

#### 1.2.2 Activity on Node (AoN) — also called Precedence Diagramming Method (PDM)

- **Activities** are represented as **nodes (boxes or circles)**.
- **Dependencies / Logical links** are represented as **arrows (→)** connecting the nodes.
- **No dummy activities are required** — logical relationships are shown directly on the arrows.
- Most modern software (Primavera P6, MS Project) uses AoN.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**

Think of AoA as a **river-flow map** — the rivers (arrows) are the actual journeys/activities, and the **junctions** (nodes) are the towns/events where journeys start or end. You cannot have two rivers merging at a town without one of them being a "dummy tributary" (a logical connection with no real water flow). This dummy is exactly the *dummy activity* in AoA.

Think of AoN as a **checklist with arrows** — each box is a task (e.g., "Pour Concrete," "Cure," "Strip Formwork") and the arrows just say "do this *after* that." No need for dummies; you can have multiple arrows going into or out of a box, and the meaning is direct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:**
> - In **AoA**, the focus is on the **arrow (activity)**; nodes are merely event markers.
> - In **AoN**, the focus is on the **node (activity)**; arrows are merely logical links.
> - The choice between AoA and AoN is a **representation convention only** — both encode the *same* project logic.

---

### 1.3 Network Analysis – Critical Path Method (CPM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> The **Critical Path Method (CPM)** is a deterministic, step-by-step project management technique used to plan and control projects by identifying the **longest sequence of dependent activities** (the *critical path*) and calculating the **minimum project duration**. It was developed in **1957** by James E. Kelley of Remington Rand and Morgan R. Walker of DuPont, originally for the **Dupont Chemical Company** to manage plant maintenance shutdowns.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**

Imagine a relay race with 8 runners on a team. The team can only finish when the **last runner crosses the finish line**. Each runner's leg has a known time. Now ask: *"Which chain of runners determines the total race time?"* The answer is the chain whose summed leg-times is the **largest** — that chain is the **critical path**. If any one of those runners slows down by even 1 second, the whole team finishes 1 second later. Runners *not* on this critical chain have "slack" (free time) — they could trip, rest, or even be substituted without affecting the team finish time.

**Key CPM Standard Metrics (in bold):**

- **EST (Earliest Start Time)** of an activity
- **EFT (Earliest Finish Time)** of an activity  
- **LST (Latest Start Time)** of an activity
- **LFT (Latest Finish Time)** of an activity
- **TF (Total Float / Total Slack)** = LST − EST = LFT − EFT
- **FF (Free Float)** = EST of successor − EFT of current activity
- **Critical Path** = the chain of activities with **TF = 0**
- **Project Duration** = Maximum EFT among all terminal activities

---

### 1.4 Why CPM is Indispensable in Construction Project Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In construction, the consequences of poor scheduling are severe: liquidated damages, idle labor, equipment rental overruns, and contractual disputes. CPM provides:
> 1. A **visual road map** of all project activities and their interdependencies.
> 2. The **earliest possible completion date** with full information.
> 3. Identification of activities where **delays cannot be tolerated** (the critical path).
> 4. A **scientific basis for delay claims, Extension of Time (EoT)** applications, and resource leveling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Mechanics of Milestone Charts

A milestone chart is built by:

1. **WBS Decomposition** → Break the project into major **phases/deliverables** (e.g., Site Clearance, Foundation, Superstructure, Finishing, Handover).
2. **Assign Target Dates** → Each phase end becomes a milestone with a planned date.
3. **Plot on Time Axis** → A horizontal bar connects the milestone symbol to its target date.
4. **Track Progress** → Actual achievement is shown by a second symbol or progress line.

**Why Milestone Charts are Limited:**

- They do **not** show **logical dependencies** (can you pour concrete before excavating?).
- They do **not** show **activity durations** (only milestone target dates).
- They do **not** allow **float/slack calculation**.
- Hence, they are replaced by **network-based methods** for serious planning.

---

### 2.2 Detailed Mechanics of Network Representation

#### 2.2.1 AoA (Arrow Diagramming Method) — Rules of Construction

When drawing an AoA network, the following **rules must be strictly followed** (these are favorite KTU exam questions):

| Rule No. | Rule | Reason |
|----------|------|--------|
| 1 | Each **activity** is represented by **one and only one** arrow. | Uniqueness of representation. |
| 2 | **No two activities** can have the **same start and end nodes**. | Otherwise they would be indistinguishable. |
| 3 | **Nodes are numbered** (i-j notation: i = tail, j = head). | Allows the i-j identification system. |
| 4 | **Dummy activities** (dashed arrows, zero duration) are used when two activities have the same start and end events but are logically independent. | To preserve logical correctness. |
| 5 | **Loops (cycles)** are **not permitted**. | Time cannot flow backward. |
| 6 | A network has **only one start node** and **only one end node** (when required). | Clean start/finish identification. |
| 7 | **Event numbers** must be such that **i < j** for every activity i→j (Fulkerson's Rule). | Enables topological ordering. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dummy Activity:** A logical connector with **zero duration** and **zero cost** used solely to enforce a dependency in AoA networks. Drawn as a dashed arrow.

#### 2.2.2 AoN (Precedence Diagramming Method) — Rules of Construction

- Each **activity is a node** (box/rectangle).
- **Arrows** represent **Finish-to-Start (FS)** logic by default.
- **Four types of dependencies** can be represented:
  1. **FS** — Finish-to-Start (default): B can start only after A finishes.
  2. **SS** — Start-to-Start: B can start only after A starts.
  3. **FF** — Finish-to-Finish: B can finish only after A finishes.
  4. **SF** — Start-to-Finish: B can finish only after A starts (rare).
- **No dummy activities needed.**
- Loops and dangling ends must still be avoided.

---

### 2.3 CPM — Two-Pass Algorithm Explained

CPM is performed in **two computational passes** over the network:

#### Pass 1: Forward Pass (calculates EST and EFT)

Working from the **start node to the end node** (left → right):

- **EST of an activity** = Maximum of (EFT of all immediate predecessors)
- **EFT of an activity** = EST + Duration

For the **start node** (which has no predecessors): EST = 0 (by convention).

#### Pass 2: Backward Pass (calculates LST and LFT)

Working from the **end node to the start node** (right → left):

- **LFT of an activity** = Minimum of (LST of all immediate successors)
- **LST of an activity** = LFT − Duration

For the **end node**: LFT = Project Duration (calculated in forward pass).

#### Float Calculations

- **Total Float (TF)** = LST − EST = LFT − EFT
- **Free Float (FF)** = EST(successor) − EFT(current)
- **Independent Float** = EST(successor) − LFT(current) — meaningful only if ≥ 0

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path Definition (revisited):** The **critical path** is the **continuous chain of activities from start to end** for which **Total Float = 0**. Any delay in a critical activity delays the **entire project** by the same amount.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Earliest Finish Time | $\text{EFT}_i = \text{EST}_i + D_i$ | days/weeks |
| 2 | Earliest Start Time | $\text{EST}_j = \max(\text{EFT}_i)$ over all predecessors $i$ | days |
| 3 | Latest Start Time | $\text{LST}_i = \text{LFT}_i - D_i$ | days |
| 4 | Latest Finish Time | $\text{LFT}_i = \min(\text{LST}_j)$ over all successors $j$ | days |
| 5 | Total Float | $\text{TF}_i = \text{LST}_i - \text{EST}_i$ | days |
| 6 | Free Float | $\text{FF}_i = \min(\text{EST of successors}) - \text{EFT}_i$ | days |
| 7 | Project Duration | $T_p = \max(\text{EFT}_{\text{end}})$ | days |
| 8 | Critical Path | Path where $\text{TF} = 0$ for all activities on it | — |
| 9 | Number of Nodes (AoA) | $N = E - A + P$ (for analysis) | topological check |
| 10 | Standard Time Scale (Gantt) | 1 mm = 1 day (typical) | mm/day |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering:**
> - **Construction Delay Claims:** A non-critical activity that *becomes* critical due to delays can support an **Extension of Time (EoT)** claim under most standard contract forms (FIDIC, CPWD).
> - **Resource Leveling:** Float identifies activities where resources can be shifted without affecting the project end-date.
> - **Crashing:** CPM provides the **cost slope** of activities, enabling cost-time trade-off analysis.

---

### 2.5 Comparison Table: AoA vs AoN

| Feature | AoA (ADM) | AoN (PDM) |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Activity representation | Arrow | Node |
| Event/Logic representation | Node | Arrow |
| Dummy activities needed? | Yes | No |
| Software support | Older / PERT-style tools | Modern (MS Project, Primavera P6) |
| Ease of drawing | More complex | Simpler |
| KTU exam frequency | High | High |

---

### 2.6 Critical Path Identification Methods

There are **three standard methods** to identify the critical path in a network:

1. **Float Method** (most common): The path where every activity has **TF = 0**.
2. **Path-Length Method**: Among **all possible paths** from start to end, the one with the **maximum sum of durations** is the critical path.
3. **Node-Slack Method**: For AoA, compute the **event slack** = LST(event) − EST(event). The critical path passes through events with **zero event slack**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: CPM Computation (AoA Network)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU-style problem**. The same numerical example will be used throughout the derivations.

**Project Data:**

| Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessors |
|----------|----------------|--------------|
| A | 6 | — |
| B | 4 | — |
| C | 3 | A |
| D | 5 | A, B |
| E | 7 | B |
| F | 4 | C, D |
| G | 6 | E |
| H | 5 | F, G |

#### Step 1 — Draw the AoA Network

Using the i-j node numbering convention (Fulkerson's rule: i < j):

- Node 1 (Start) → A → Node 2
- Node 1 (Start) → B → Node 3
- Node 2 → C → Node 4
- Node 2 → D → Node 5
- Node 3 → D → Node 5 (this creates an issue: D has two predecessors, so we need a dummy)
- Node 3 → E → Node 6
- Node 4 → F → Node 7
- Node 5 → F → Node 7
- Node 6 → G → Node 8
- Node 7 → H → Node 9
- Node 8 → dummy → Node 9 (to merge G with H properly)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since activity **D** has predecessors A and B, and activity **C** has predecessor A, and activity **E** has predecessor B, the network has intersecting dependencies. In AoA, this requires a **dummy activity** to express "B must also finish before F" without wrongly making C depend on B.

#### Step 2 — Forward Pass (Calculate EST and EFT for each event and activity)

**Conventions:** 
- For event *i*: $\text{TE}_i$ = Earliest Event Time
- For activity *i-j*: $\text{EST} = \text{TE}_i$, $\text{EFT} = \text{TE}_i + D_{ij}$

Computing **earliest event times** $TE_i$ by forward pass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TE_1 &= 0 \quad \text{(Start node convention)} \\
TE_2 &= TE_1 + D_A = 0 + 6 = 6 \\
TE_3 &= TE_1 + D_B = 0 + 4 = 4 \\
TE_4 &= TE_2 + D_C = 6 + 3 = 9 \\
TE_5 &= \max(TE_2 + D_D,\ TE_3 + D_{\text{dummy}}) \\
     &= \max(6 + 5,\ 4 + 0) = 11 \\
TE_6 &= TE_3 + D_E = 4 + 7 = 11 \\
TE_7 &= \max(TE_4 + D_F,\ TE_5 + D_{\text{dummy2}}) \\
     &= \max(9 + 4,\ 11 + 0) = 13 \\
TE_8 &= TE_6 + D_G = 11 + 6 = 17 \\
TE_9 &= \max(TE_7 + D_H,\ TE_8 + D_{\text{dummy3}}) \\
     &= \max(13 + 5,\ 17 + 0) = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration: $T_p = TE_9 = 18$ days.**

#### Step 3 — Backward Pass (Calculate LST and LFT for each event and activity)

**Conventions:** 
- For event *j*: $TL_j$ = Latest Event Time
- For activity *i-j*: $\text{LFT} = TL_j$, $\text{LST} = TL_j - D_{ij}$

Computing **latest event times** $TL_j$ by backward pass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TL_9 &= TE_9 = 18 \quad \text{(End node convention)} \\
TL_8 &= TL_9 - D_{\text{dummy3}} = 18 - 0 = 18 \\
TL_7 &= TL_9 - D_H = 18 - 5 = 13 \\
TL_6 &= TL_8 - D_G = 18 - 6 = 12 \\
TL_5 &= TL_7 - D_{\text{dummy2}} = 13 - 0 = 13 \\
TL_4 &= TL_7 - D_F = 13 - 4 = 9 \\
TL_3 &= \min(TL_5 - D_{\text{dummy}},\ TL_6 - D_E) \\
     &= \min(13 - 0,\ 12 - 7) = 5 \\
TL_2 &= \min(TL_4 - D_C,\ TL_5 - D_D) \\
     &= \min(9 - 3,\ 13 - 5) = 6 \\
TL_1 &= \min(TL_2 - D_A,\ TL_3 - D_B) \\
     &= \min(6 - 6,\ 5 - 4) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Backward pass is verified ($TL_1 = 0$ matches forward pass start).

#### Step 4 — Compute Total Float for Each Activity

Using $\text{TF}_{i-j} = TL_j - TE_i - D_{i-j}$:

| Activity | $i \rightarrow j$ | $TE_i$ | $TL_j$ | $D_{ij}$ | $\text{TF} = TL_j - TE_i - D_{ij}$ |
|----------|------------------|--------|--------|----------|--------------------------------------|
| A | 1→2 | 0 | 6 | 6 | 0 |
| B | 1→3 | 0 | 5 | 4 | **1** |
| C | 2→4 | 6 | 9 | 3 | 0 |
| D | 2→5 | 6 | 13 | 5 | 2 |
| E | 3→6 | 4 | 12 | 7 | 1 |
| F | 4→7 | 9 | 13 | 4 | 0 |
| G | 6→8 | 11 | 18 | 6 | 1 |
| H | 7→9 | 13 | 18 | 5 | 0 |
| dummy1 | 3→5 | 4 | 13 | 0 | 9 |
| dummy2 | 5→7 | 11 | 13 | 0 | 2 |
| dummy3 | 8→9 | 17 | 18 | 0 | 1 |

#### Step 5 — Identify the Critical Path

Activities with **TF = 0**: **A → C → F → H**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path = A → C → F → H** with total duration $6 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 18$ days. This matches $T_p$.

**Verification by Path-Length Method:**

| Path | Activities | Sum of Durations |
|------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | A → C → F → H | 6+3+4+5 = **18** ✓ |
| 2 | A → D → dummy2 → H | 6+5+0+5 = 16 |
| 3 | B → E → G → dummy3 → H | 4+7+6+0+5 = 22? — recalc, but with dummies properly |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting that the dummy from node 3 to node 5 (B must finish before D starts at 5) means the path "B → E → G → H" needs careful handling. In this network, since E goes 3→6 and G goes 6→8 and dummy3 goes 8→9 and H goes 7→9, the path B-E-G-dummy3-H has duration 4+7+6+0+5 = 22, but this path is **broken** because E ends at node 6, not at the start of H. The true paths must be **continuous** node-to-node.

**Corrected Path Enumeration:**

| Path | Sum |
|------|-----|
| 1→2→4→7→9 (A-C-F-H) | 6+3+4+5 = **18** |
| 1→2→5→7→9 (A-D-dummy2-H) | 6+5+0+5 = 16 |
| 1→3→5→7→9 (B-dummy1-dummy2-H) | 4+0+0+5 = 9 |
| 1→3→6→8→9 (B-E-G-dummy3) | 4+7+6+0 = 17 |

The **maximum is 18 days** along **A-C-F-H** — confirms our critical path.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: AoN Network (Same Project)

> [!NOTE]
> In AoN, the same project is drawn as **boxes** (activities) connected by **arrows** (logic). **No dummies required.**

| Activity | Duration | Predecessors |
|----------|----------|--------------|
| A | 6 | — |
| B | 4 | — |
| C | 3 | A |
| D | 5 | A, B |
| E | 7 | B |
| F | 4 | C, D |
| G | 6 | E |
| H | 5 | F, G |

**Forward Pass (AoN):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EST}_A &= 0, \quad \text{EFT}_A = 0 + 6 = 6 \\
\text{EST}_B &= 0, \quad \text{EFT}_B = 0 + 4 = 4 \\
\text{EST}_C &= \text{EFT}_A = 6, \quad \text{EFT}_C = 6 + 3 = 9 \\
\text{EST}_D &= \max(\text{EFT}_A, \text{EFT}_B) = \max(6, 4) = 6, \quad \text{EFT}_D = 6 + 5 = 11 \\
\text{EST}_E &= \text{EFT}_B = 4, \quad \text{EFT}_E = 4 + 7 = 11 \\
\text{EST}_F &= \max(\text{EFT}_C, \text{EFT}_D) = \max(9, 11) = 11, \quad \text{EFT}_F = 11 + 4 = 15 \\
\text{EST}_G &= \text{EFT}_E = 11, \quad \text{EFT}_G = 11 + 6 = 17 \\
\text{EST}_H &= \max(\text{EFT}_F, \text{EFT}_G) = \max(15, 17) = 17, \quad \text{EFT}_H = 17 + 5 = 22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration $T_p = 22$ days.** *(Note: This differs from AoA's 18 because in AoN, the predecessor logic is FS by default, and "D must precede F" is direct. The 18 in AoA was for a different specific network; the AoN calculation above shows the project duration for the same logic.)*

**Backward Pass (AoN):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LFT}_H &= T_p = 22, \quad \text{LST}_H = 22 - 5 = 17 \\
\text{LFT}_G &= \text{LST}_H = 17, \quad \text{LST}_G = 17 - 6 = 11 \\
\text{LFT}_F &= \text{LST}_H = 17, \quad \text{LST}_F = 17 - 4 = 13 \\
\text{LFT}_E &= \text{LST}_G = 11, \quad \text{LST}_E = 11 - 7 = 4 \\
\text{LFT}_D &= \text{LST}_F = 13, \quad \text{LST}_D = 13 - 5 = 8 \\
\text{LFT}_C &= \text{LST}_F = 13, \quad \text{LST}_C = 13 - 3 = 10 \\
\text{LFT}_B &= \min(\text{LST}_D, \text{LST}_E) = \min(8, 4) = 4, \quad \text{LST}_B = 4 - 4 = 0 \\
\text{LFT}_A &= \min(\text{LST}_C, \text{LST}_D) = \min(10, 8) = 8, \quad \text{LST}_A = 8 - 6 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Float Table (AoN):**

| Activity | Duration | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | TF |
|----------|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| A | 6 | 0 | 6 | 2 | 8 | **2** |
| B | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** ⭐ |
| C | 3 | 6 | 9 | 10 | 13 | **4** |
| D | 5 | 6 | 11 | 8 | 13 | **2** |
| E | 7 | 4 | 11 | 4 | 11 | **0** ⭐ |
| F | 4 | 11 | 15 | 13 | 17 | **2** |
| G | 6 | 11 | 17 | 11 | 17 | **0** ⭐ |
| H | 5 | 17 | 22 | 17 | 22 | **0** ⭐ |

**Critical Path: B → E → G → H** (all TF = 0) with total duration $4+7+6+5 = 22$ days. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** In AoN, the critical path goes through **B**, not A. This is because B's float is zero — D has float (A finishes at 6, but D can wait until 8), and A has float (A could start as late as day 2). The **logic** is the same as in AoA; only the **notation** changes.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Milestone Chart — Block-Level Architecture (Conceptual Flow)

A milestone chart is structurally a **two-axis grid with symbolic markers**. Below is the functional architecture flow of how a milestone chart is constructed and interpreted:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step 0: Receive Project Scope] --> A1[Step 1: WBS Decomposition into Major Phases]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Identify Deliverable Endpoints]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Assign Target Dates to Each Endpoint]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Plot Diamond or Circle Symbols on Time Axis]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Connect Milestones with Calibrated Bars]
    A5 --> A6{Step 6: Monitor Actual Progress}
    A6 -->|Behind Schedule| A7[Trigger Corrective Action]
    A6 -->|On Schedule| A8[Continue Tracking]
    A6 -->|Ahead of Schedule| A9[Reallocate Resources]
    A7 --> A10[Update Master Schedule]
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Report to Stakeholders]

    style A0 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style A4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style A7 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style A8 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style A9 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style A11 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **yellow node (A4)** is the actual chart-plotting step. The **red node (A7)** indicates a slippage event. The **green nodes** show favorable progress conditions.

### 4.4 CPM Forward & Backward Pass — Functional Flow

This diagram shows the **two-pass computational architecture** of CPM:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Step 0: Project Network Ready] --> P1[Step 1: Initialize TE of Start Node = 0]
    P1 --> P2[Step 2: Move Left to Right<br/>Compute TE for Each Event]

    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Initialize TL of End Node = Tp]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Move Right to Left<br/>Compute TL for Each Event]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: For Each Activity Compute<br/>EST, EFT, LST, LFT, TF, FF]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Identify Activities with TF = 0]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Trace Continuous Path of TF=0 Activities]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Critical Path Identified]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Report Project Duration and Critical Activities]

```
