# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Quantity Takeoff for a Single-Room Building (G+0)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU Standard Pattern)

A residential room has the following internal dimensions: **Length = 5.0 m, Width = 4.0 m, Height = 3.0 m**. Wall thickness = **0.30 m (external) and 0.115 m (internal partition, ignored for this example)**. Foundation width = **0.9 m**, depth below GL = **1.0 m**. PCC thickness = **0.10 m**. Plinth height = **0.5 m** above GL. One door (1.0 × 2.1 m), one window (1.2 × 1.2 m).

Compute:
1. Earthwork in excavation
2. PCC in foundation (1:4:8)
3. Brickwork in foundation & plinth
4. Brickwork in superstructure
5. RCC in roof slab (assume 0.12 m thick, total area = 5.6 × 4.6 m²)
6. Cement required for superstructure brickwork (mortar 1:6, $V_{mortar} = 0.20 \times V_{brickwork}$)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute wall lengths**

External wall length (long wall) = internal length + 2 × wall thickness = $5.0 + 2 \times 0.3 = 5.6$ m
External wall length (short wall) = internal width + wall thickness = $4.0 + 0.3 = 4.3$ m
Total wall length (perimeter, single circuit) = $2 \times (5.6 + 4.3) = 19.8$ m

**Step 2: Earthwork in excavation**

$$V_{exc} = L \times B \times D = 19.8 \times 0.9 \times 1.0 = 17.82 \text{ m}^3$$

Add 5% wastage:

$$V_{exc, \text{final}} = 17.82 \times 1.05 = 18.711 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: PCC in foundation**

$$V_{PCC} = L \times B \times t = 19.8 \times 0.9 \times 0.10 = 1.782 \text{ m}^3$$

Add 5% wastage:

$$V_{PCC, \text{final}} = 1.782 \times 1.05 = 1.8711 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Brickwork in foundation & plinth**

Wall thickness at foundation level = 0.3 m, height = 0.5 m (plinth) + 0.3 m (foundation stem up to GL) = assume 0.8 m total below GL + 0.5 m plinth = 1.3 m height. **But for foundation brickwork, height is from top of PCC to GL = 0.5 m. Then plinth brickwork from GL to floor level = 0.5 m. Total = 1.0 m.**

$$V_{brickwork, fp} = 19.8 \times 0.3 \times 1.0 = 5.94 \text{ m}^3$$

Add 5% wastage:

$$V_{brickwork, fp, \text{final}} = 5.94 \times 1.05 = 6.237 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Brickwork in superstructure**

Height = floor to ceiling = 3.0 m. Long wall = 5.6 m, short wall = 4.3 m.

Using long-wall/short-wall:

$$V_{super, long} = 5.6 \times 0.3 \times 3.0 = 5.04 \text{ m}^3$$
$$V_{super, short} = 4.3 \times 0.3 \times 3.0 = 3.87 \text{ m}^3$$
$$V_{super, total} = 5.04 + 3.87 = 8.91 \text{ m}^3$$

Deduct door and window (using wall thickness 0.3 m):

$$V_{deduction, door} = 1.0 \times 2.1 \times 0.3 = 0.63 \text{ m}^3$$
$$V_{deduction, window} = 1.2 \times 1.2 \times 0.3 = 0.432 \text{ m}^3$$
$$V_{super, net} = 8.91 - 0.63 - 0.432 = 7.848 \text{ m}^3$$

Add 5% wastage:

$$V_{super, \text{final}} = 7.848 \times 1.05 = 8.2404 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6: RCC roof slab**

$$V_{RCC, slab} = 5.6 \times 4.6 \times 0.12 = 3.0912 \text{ m}^3$$

Add 5% wastage:

$$V_{RCC, slab, \text{final}} = 3.0912 \times 1.05 = 3.24576 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 7: Dry volume of materials for slab**

$$V_{dry, slab} = 1.52 \times 3.0912 = 4.6986 \text{ m}^3$$

For M20 mix (1:1.5:3, total parts = 5.5):

$$V_{cement} = \frac{1}{5.5} \times 4.6986 = 0.8543 \text{ m}^3$$

Convert to bags (1 bag = 50 kg, density of cement = 1440 kg/m³, so 1 bag = 0.0347 m³):

$$\text{Number of bags} = \frac{0.8543}{0.0347} = 24.62 \text{ bags}$$

**Step 8: Cement for brickwork mortar (1:6)**

Volume of mortar = $0.20 \times V_{super, net} = 0.20 \times 7.848 = 1.5696 \text{ m}^3$

Add 25% for dry mortar (in practice, use 1.30 factor):

$$V_{mortar, dry} = 1.30 \times 1.5696 = 2.0405 \text{ m}^3$$

For 1:6 (cement:sand, total parts = 7):

$$V_{cement} = \frac{1}{7} \times 2.0405 = 0.2915 \text{ m}^3$$

Convert to bags:

$$\text{Number of bags} = \frac{0.2915}{0.0347} = 8.40 \text{ bags}$$

### 3.1.3 Final BOQ Summary Table

| Sl. | Item | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation | 18.71 | m³ | 320 | 5,987 |
| 2 | PCC 1:4:8 in foundation | 1.87 | m³ | 6,500 | 12,155 |
| 3 | Brickwork in foundation & plinth | 6.24 | m³ | 7,200 | 44,928 |
| 4 | Brickwork in superstructure (1:6) | 8.24 | m³ | 8,000 | 65,920 |
| 5 | RCC M20 in roof slab | 3.25 | m³ | 9,500 | 30,875 |
| 6 | Steel in slab (1.2% of concrete) | 306 | kg | 75 | 22,950 |
| 7 | Cement for slab (24.62 bags) | 24.62 | bags | 420 | 10,340 |
| 8 | Cement for super mortar (8.40 bags) | 8.40 | bags | 420 | 3,528 |
| | **Subtotal** | | | | **₹ 1,96,683** |
| | Add 5% Contingency | | | | 9,834 |
| | **Grand Total** | | | | **₹ 2,06,517** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: CPM Analysis for a 7-Activity Network

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A small construction project has the following activities, durations, and dependencies:

| Activity | Description | Duration (days) | Predecessor |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Site clearance | 2 | — |
| B | Excavation | 3 | A |
| C | PCC and foundation | 4 | B |
| D | Brickwork up to plinth | 5 | C |
| E | RCC columns and beams | 6 | C |
| F | Brickwork in superstructure | 8 | D, E |
| G | Roof slab and finishing | 5 | F |

Find: ES, EF, LS, LF, TF, FF, Critical Path, Project Duration.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution (Forward Pass)

**Step 1: Identify the predecessors of each activity**

- A: —
- B: A
- C: B
- D: C
- E: C
- F: D, E
- G: F

**Step 2: Compute ES and EF (forward pass from Day 0)**

| Activity | Duration $D_i$ | ES (earliest start) | EF (earliest finish) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 0 | 0 + 2 = 2 |
| B | 3 | EF(A) = 2 | 2 + 3 = 5 |
| C | 4 | EF(B) = 5 | 5 + 4 = 9 |
| D | 5 | EF(C) = 9 | 9 + 5 = 14 |
| E | 6 | EF(C) = 9 | 9 + 6 = 15 |
| F | 8 | max(EF(D), EF(E)) = max(14, 15) = 15 | 15 + 8 = 23 |
| G | 5 | EF(F) = 23 | 23 + 5 = 28 |

**Project Duration = max(EF) = 28 days**

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Solution (Backward Pass)

Starting from the end (LF of last activity G = project duration = 28):

| Activity | Duration $D_i$ | LF (latest finish) | LS (latest start) |
|---|---|---|---|
| G | 5 | 28 | 28 - 5 = 23 |
| F | 8 | LS(G) = 23 | 23 - 8 = 15 |
| E | 6 | LS(F) = 15 | 15 - 6 = 9 |
| D | 5 | LS(F) = 15 | 15 - 5 = 10 |
| C | 4 | min(LS(D), LS(E)) = min(10, 9) = 9 | 9 - 4 = 5 |
| B | 3 | LS(C) = 5 | 5 - 3 = 2 |
| A | 2 | LS(B) = 2 | 2 - 2 = 0 |

### 3.2.4 Step-by-Step Solution (Float Calculation)

| Activity | D | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF = LS - ES | FF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 2 | **0** | min(ES(B)) - EF(A) = 2 - 2 = 0 |
| B | 3 | 2 | 5 | 2 | 5 | **0** | 5 - 5 = 0 |
| C | 4 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 9 | **0** | 5 - 9 = -4... wait, recompute |

Let me carefully recompute FF using the correct definition:

$$FF_i = \min_{k \in \text{succ}(i)} ES_k - EF_i$$

| Activity | EF | Successor ES | FF |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | ES(B) = 2 | 2 - 2 = **0** |
| B | 5 | ES(C) = 5 | 5 - 5 = **0** |
| C | 9 | min(ES(D)=9, ES(E)=9) = 9 | 9 - 9 = **0** |
| D | 14 | ES(F) = 15 | 15 - 14 = **1** |
| E | 15 | ES(F) = 15 | 15 - 15 = **0** |
| F | 23 | ES(G) = 23 | 23 - 23 = **0** |
| G | 28 | — | — |

### 3.2.5 Summary Table

| Activity | D | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| B | 3 | 2 | 5 | 2 | 5 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| C | 4 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 9 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| D | 5 | 9 | 14 | 10 | 15 | 1 | 1 | ✗ |
| E | 6 | 9 | 15 | 9 | 15 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| F | 8 | 15 | 23 | 15 | 23 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| G | 5 | 23 | 28 | 23 | 28 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |

**Critical Path = A → B → C → E → F → G (Project Duration = 28 days)**

**Inference**: Activity D has TF = FF = 1 day, meaning its start can be delayed by 1 day without affecting the project. This is a **near-critical activity** that requires close monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight**: When TF = FF, the activity is **independent** (no shared float). When TF > FF, the activity is on a path that shares float with another path. Both cases are important for exam answers.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Resource Histogram Construction

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

For the same project (28 days), suppose the labor coefficients (mason-days per activity) are:

| Activity | Masons Required | Carpenters Required | Helpers Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 0 | 4 |
| B | 0 | 0 | 6 |
| C | 2 | 0 | 4 |
| D | 4 | 0 | 4 |
| E | 4 | 6 | 6 |
| F | 6 | 0 | 4 |
| G | 4 | 6 | 4 |

Construct a daily labor histogram (masons only).

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Distribute the labor uniformly across each activity's duration**

Daily mason demand for activity = (Total masons) / (Duration)

| Activity | Total Masons | Duration | Masons per Day |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 2 | 0 |
| B | 0 | 3 | 0 |
| C | 2 | 4 | 0.5 |
| D | 4 | 5 | 0.8 |
| E | 4 | 6 | 0.667 |
| F | 6 | 8 | 0.75 |
| G | 4 | 5 | 0.8 |

**Step 2: Sum the daily mason demand for each day**

| Day Range | Active Activities | Masons per Day |
|---|---|---|
| Day 1-2 (A) | A | 0 |
| Day 3-5 (B) | B | 0 |
| Day 6-9 (C) | C | 0.5 |
| Day 10-14 (D, E) | D + E | 0.8 + 0.667 = 1.467 |
| Day 15-22 (E, F) | E + F | 0.667 + 0.75 = 1.417 |
| Day 23-28 (G) | G | 0.8 |

**Step 3: Identify peak and average**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{\sum \text{daily masons}}{28} = \frac{(0 \times 2) + (0 \times 3) + (0.5 \times 4) + (1.467 \times 5) + (1.417 \times 8) + (0.8 \times 5)}{28}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{0 + 0 + 2.0 + 7.335 + 11.336 + 4.0}{28} = \frac{24.671}{28} = 0.881 \text{ masons/day}$$

$$R_{max} = 1.467 \text{ masons (on Day 10-14)}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

The peak demand is during **foundation + column casting** (Day 10-14). In practice, a contractor would deploy **2 masons** (round up) from Day 6 to Day 28, with flexibility on non-peak days. The histogram helps the contractor decide on **hiring vs. subcontracting**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: CPM Forward and Backward Pass — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FP["FORWARD PASS - Compute ES and EF"]
        fp1["Start from Day 0"] --> fp2["For each activity ES equals max of predecessor EF"]
        fp2 --> fp3["Compute EF equals ES plus Duration"]
        fp3 --> fp4["Project Duration equals max of all EF"]
    end
    
    subgraph BP["BACKWARD PASS - Compute LS and LF"]
        bp1["Start from LF of last activity equals Project Duration"] --> bp2["For each activity LF equals min of successor LS"]
        bp2 --> bp3["Compute LS equals LF minus Duration"]
    end
    
    subgraph FL["FLOAT COMPUTATION"]
        fl1["TF equals LS minus ES equals LF minus EF"] --> fl2["Critical Path consists of activities with TF equals 0"]
        fl2 --> fl3["FF equals min successor ES minus EF"]
    end
    
    FP --> BP
    BP --> FL
    FL --> output(["Critical Path, Project Duration, Floats Identified"])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"List the five mandatory phases of the KTU PBL module for Construction Project Management."**

**Model Answer:**

The five mandatory phases of the KTU PBL module for Construction Project Management are:
1. **Drafting Phase**: Develop basic architectural and structural drawings (Plan, Elevation, Section, Site Plan) of a G+1 residential/small commercial building using AutoCAD/Revit.
2. **Quantity Estimation Phase**: Approximate estimation of material quantities and rates, culminating in a Bill of Quantities (BOQ) using Excel/Google Sheets.
3. **Scheduling Phase**: Develop a Gantt Chart or Precedence Network Diagram, identify the Critical Path, and compute Total/Free Floats using MS Project/Primavera.
4. **Resource Scheduling Phase**: Develop a resource schedule (labor, material, equipment) over the project duration.
5. **Report Submission Phase**: Consolidate all artifacts into a Project Planning Report and submit as a ZIP file (PDF + DWG + XLSX + MPP).

> [!TIP]
> Use bullet points and bold the software names for **maximum readability and full marks**.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"What is the Critical Path in a CPM network? How is it identified?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **Critical Path** is the longest path (in terms of total duration) through a project network, which determines the **minimum time** required to complete the project. It is identified by:
1. Performing a **Forward Pass** to compute ES and EF for all activities.
2. Performing a **Backward Pass** to compute LS and LF for all activities.
3. Calculating **Total Float (TF = LS − ES)** for each activity.
4. The activities with **TF = 0** form the **critical path**.
5. Any delay in a critical activity **directly delays** the project completion by the same amount of time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A (Option A) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**"For a proposed G+1 residential building with the following data, prepare: (a) A preliminary Bill of Quantities (BOQ) using long-wall/short-wall method, and (b) A network diagram and identify the critical path."**

**Given Data:**

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| Building Type | G+1 Residential |
| Ground Floor Plan (internal) | 8.0 m × 6.0 m |
| First Floor Plan (internal) | 8.0 m × 6.0 m |
| Floor-to-floor height | 3.0 m |
| External wall thickness | 0.30 m |
| Plinth height above GL | 0.5 m |
| Foundation depth below GL | 1.0 m |
| Foundation width | 0.9 m |
| PCC thickness | 0.10 m |
| Roof slab thickness | 0.12 m |
| Doors | 4 nos. (1.0 m × 2.1 m) |
| Windows | 6 nos. (1.2 m × 1.2 m) |

**Activity List for Scheduling:**

| Activity | Description | Duration (days) | Predecessor |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Site clearance and layout | 3 | — |
| B | Excavation | 5 | A |
| C | PCC and foundation | 6 | B |
| D | Plinth brickwork | 7 | C |
| E | Ground floor columns | 8 | C |
| F | Ground floor brickwork | 10 | D, E |
| G | First floor slab | 8 | F |
| H | First floor brickwork | 10 | G |
| I | Roof slab | 6 | H |
| J | Plastering and finishing | 8 | I |

#### Part (a) — BOQ (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute wall lengths**

Long wall (per floor) = $8.0 + 2 \times 0.3 = 8.6$ m
Short wall (per floor) = $6.0 + 0.3 = 6.3$ m
Total perimeter per floor = $2 \times (8.6 + 6.3) = 29.8$ m

**Step 2: Earthwork in excavation**

$V_{exc} = 29.8 \times 0.9 \times 1.0 = 26.82$ m³
With 5% wastage: $V_{exc, final} = 28.16$ m³

**Step 3: PCC in foundation**

$V_{PCC} = 29.8 \times 0.9 \times 0.10 = 2.682$ m³
With 5% wastage: $V_{PCC, final} = 2.816$ m³

**Step 4: Brickwork in foundation & plinth**

Height = 0.5 (plinth) + 0.5 (foundation stem up to GL) = 1.0 m
$V_{fp} = 29.8 \times 0.3 \times 1.0 = 8.94$ m³
With 5% wastage: $V_{fp, final} = 9.387$ m³

**Step 5: Ground floor brickwork (superstructure)**

Long wall = 8.6 m, Short wall = 6.3 m, Height = 3.0 m
$V_{long} = 8.6 \times 0.3 \times 3.0 = 7.74$ m³
$V_{short} = 6.3 \times 0.3 \times 3.0 = 5.67$ m³
$V_{total} = 13.41$ m³

Deductions:
- 2 doors on ground floor: $2 \times 1.0 \times 2.1 \times 0.3 = 1.26$ m³
- 3 windows on ground floor: $3 \times 1.2 \times 1.2 \times 0.3 = 1.296$ m³
- Total deduction: 2.556 m³

$V_{gf, net} = 13.41 - 2.556 = 10.854$ m³
With 5% wastage: $V_{gf, final} = 11.397$ m³

**Step 6: First floor brickwork (same as ground floor, with 2 doors + 3 windows)**

$V_{ff, net} = 10.854$ m³
With 5% wastage: $V_{ff, final} = 11.397$ m³

**Step 7: Roof slab and first floor slab (RCC M20)**

Slab area (per slab) = $8.6 \times 6.3 = 54.18$ m²
$V_{slab, ff} = 54.18 \times 0.12 = 6.50$ m³
$V_{slab, roof} = 54.18 \times 0.12 = 6.50$ m³
Total RCC in slabs = 13.00 m³
With 5% wastage: $V_{slab, final} = 13.65$ m³

**Step 8: BOQ Summary Table**

| Sl. | Item | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation | 28.16 | m³ | 320 | 9,011 |
| 2 | PCC 1:4:8 in foundation | 2.82 | m³ | 6,500 | 18,330 |
| 3 | Brickwork in foundation & plinth (1:6) | 9.39 | m³ | 7,200 | 67,608 |
| 4 | Ground floor brickwork (1:6) | 11.40 | m³ | 8,000 | 91,200 |
| 5 | First floor brickwork (1:6) | 11.40 | m³ | 8,000 | 91,200 |
| 6 | RCC M20 in slabs (FF + Roof) | 13.65 | m³ | 9,500 | 1,29,675 |
| 7 | Steel (1.2% of concrete) | 1,285 | kg | 75 | 96,375 |
| 8 | Plastering (internal + external) | 850 | m² | 280 | 2,38,000 |
| | **Subtotal** | | | | **₹ 7,41,399** |
| | Add 5% Contingency | | | | 37,070 |
| | Add 18% GST | | | | 1,40,024 |
| | **Grand Total** | | | | **₹ 9,18,493** |

#### Part (b) — Network Diagram and Critical Path (7 marks)

**Forward Pass (ES, EF):**

| Activity | D | ES | EF |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 3 | 0 | 3 |
| B | 5 | 3 | 8 |
| C | 6 | 8 | 14 |
| D | 7 | 14 | 21 |
| E | 8 | 14 | 22 |
| F | 10 | max(21, 22) = 22 | 32 |
| G | 8 | 32 | 40 |
| H | 10 | 40 | 50 |
| I | 6 | 50 | 56 |
| J | 8 | 56 | 64 |

**Project Duration = 64 days**

**Backward Pass (LS, LF):**

| Activity | D | LF | LS |
|---|---|---|---|
| J | 8 | 64 | 56 |
| I | 6 | 56 | 50 |
| H | 10 | 50 | 40 |
| G | 8 | 40 | 32 |
| F | 10 | 32 | 22 |
| E | 8 | 22 | 14 |
| D | 7 | 22 | 15 |
| C | 6 | min(15, 14) = 14 | 8 |
| B | 5 | 8 | 3 |
| A | 3 | 3 | 0 |

**Float Computation:**

| Activity | D | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| B | 5 | 3 | 8 | 3 | 8 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| C | 6 | 8 | 14 | 8 | 14 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| D | 7 | 14 | 21 | 15 | 22 | 1 | 1 | ✗ |
| E | 8 | 14 | 22 | 14 | 22 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| F | 10 | 22 | 32 | 22 | 32 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| G | 8 | 32 | 40 | 32 | 40 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| H | 10 | 40 | 50 | 40 | 50 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| I | 6 | 50 | 56 | 50 | 56 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| J | 8 | 56 | 64 | 56 | 64 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |

**Critical Path: A → B → C → E → F → G → H → I → J**

**Project Duration: 64 days**

**Inference**: Activity D has TF = FF = 1 day, so it is an **independent float activity** that can be delayed by 1 day without affecting the project completion.

### Question B-B (Option B — Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**"Explain in detail the methodology of developing a Precedence Network Diagram and a Resource Histogram for a G+1 residential building project. Use suitable activity list and software references."**

#### Part (a) — Precedence Network Methodology (7 marks)

**Answer:**

**Step 1: Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**

The entire G+1 project is decomposed into manageable activities. A typical WBS for a G+1 residential building includes:
- Substructure: Site clearance, Excavation, PCC, Foundation brickwork, Plinth beam
- Superstructure: Columns, Beams, Slabs, Brickwork
- Finishing: Plastering, Flooring, Painting, Electrical, Plumbing
- External: Compound wall, Sump, Septic tank

**Step 2: Define Activity Durations**

Each activity is assigned a duration based on quantity, productivity, and crew size. For example:
- Brickwork: 8-10 days per floor (for 8 m × 6 m plan)
- RCC slab casting: 1 day for casting + 14 days curing + 1 day deshuttering

**Step 3: Identify Dependencies**

Dependencies are categorized as:
- **Mandatory (Hard) Logic**: e.g., Brickwork must follow Column casting.
- **Discretionary (Soft) Logic**: e.g., Painting may follow Electrical wiring or vice versa.
- **External**: e.g., Material delivery, permit approval.

**Step 4: Draw the Network (AON in MS Project)**

Using **MS Project**:
- Open MSP → Create a new project → Set calendar (typically 8 hours/day, 6 days/week).
- Enter each activity in the **Task Name** column.
- Enter the **Duration** in days.
- Link predecessors using the **Predecessors** column.
- MSP automatically draws the **Gantt Chart** and **Network Diagram** (View → Network Diagram).

**Step 5: Perform CPM Analysis**

MS Project automatically computes ES, EF, LS, LF, TF, FF, and **highlights the critical path in red** on the Gantt chart.

**Step 6: Identify the Critical Path**

The red-colored activities on the Gantt chart (TF = 0) form the **critical path**. Any delay in these activities **delays the project**.

#### Part (b) — Resource Histogram Methodology (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify Resource Requirements**

For each activity, list the required resources. For example:
- **Excavation**: 1 JCB (1 day), 4 helpers (1 day)
- **Brickwork**: 4 masons + 4 helpers per day for 10 days
- **RCC casting**: 6 carpenters + 4 helpers + 1 concrete mixer (1 day)
- **Plastering**: 6 masons + 4 helpers (8 days)

**Step 2: Enter Resources in MS Project**

In MSP:
- **Resource Sheet** view → Create resources (Labor, Material, Equipment) with names, type (Work/Material/Cost), and max units.
- In the **Task Form**, assign resources to each activity by entering units (e.g., 4 masons × 100% = 4).

**Step 3: Generate Resource Histogram**

- **View** → **Resource Graph** or **Resource Usage** view.
- The histogram shows daily/weekly resource demand as bars.

**Step 4: Resource Leveling**

- **Resource** → **Level Resource** (automatic leveling tool).
- MSP redistributes activities within their floats to **smooth out peaks and valleys**.

**Step 5: Cash Flow S-Curve**

- **Report** → **Cash Flow** report in MSP.
- The S-curve shows cumulative cost over time, useful for client billing and contractor financing.

**Step 6: Interpret and Optimize**

- Identify peak demand days and arrange for additional crews or subcontractors.
- Identify low-demand days and reschedule non-critical activities to those days.
- This is called **Resource Smoothing** (within float) or **Resource Leveling** (changing critical path).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Forgetting Wastage %**: The most common mistake in BOQ. KTU expects wastage of 5% for concrete/steel/brickwork, 10% for plaster, 5% for earthwork. Missing this loses 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Incorrect Float Formula**: Students often confuse Total Float and Free Float. Remember: **TF = LS − ES** (used for critical path), **FF = min(successor ES) − EF** (used for independent delay).
> 3. **No Backward Pass**: A common error is computing only ES/EF and claiming the critical path is the "longest path visually." Always perform the **backward pass** to confirm TF = 0.
> 4. **Mixing Units**: Using cm³ instead of m³, or m² for volume, results in dimensional errors. KTU deducts **2 marks minimum** for unit inconsistency.
> 5. **Not Deductions for Openings**: In brickwork BOQ, students often forget to deduct door and window volumes. This **overestimates the quantity by 10-20%**, leading to a wrong total cost.
> 6. **Confusing AOA and AON**: AOA uses arrows for activities (dummy activities needed for parallel paths), AON uses boxes (no dummies needed). State the type clearly in the diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Bullet Points

- ✅ The **5 mandatory PBL phases** are: Drafting → Estimation/BOQ → Scheduling/CPM → Resource Scheduling → Report Submission.
- ✅ The recommended **G+1 building** scope: ground + first floor, residential or small commercial, drawn in AutoCAD/Revit.
- ✅ **Drawing scales**: Floor Plan = 1:100, Section = 1:50, Site Plan = 1:200.
- ✅ **Standard wastage allowances**: 5% for concrete/steel/brickwork, 10% for plaster, 5% for earthwork.
- ✅ **Long-wall/Short-wall method** is the most common BOQ method for KTU PBL.
- ✅ **Dry volume factor for concrete** = **1.52** (preferred) or **1.54**.
- ✅ **Cement bag volume** = 0.0347 m³ (50 kg ÷ 1440 kg/m³).
- ✅ **Steel weight** = Volume × 7850 kg/m³; for residential, **1.0-1.5% steel** by concrete volume.
- ✅ **CPM formulas**: ES = max(EF_pred); EF = ES + D; LF = min(LS_succ); LS = LF − D; TF = LS − ES; FF = min(ES_succ) − EF.
- ✅ **Critical Path = activities with TF = 0**; highlighted in red on Gantt chart.
- ✅ **Gantt Chart** = bar chart with time on X-axis, activities on Y-axis; **Network Diagram** = node-arrow representation of dependencies.
- ✅ **Resource Histogram** = bar chart of daily/weekly resource demand; **S-curve** = cumulative cost over time.
- ✅ **Float types**: TF (Total Float) is shared along a path; FF (Free Float) is independent for that activity.
- ✅ **MS Project** is the most common software for CPM; **Primavera P6** is the industry standard for large projects.
- ✅ **Submission format**: ZIP file containing PDF report + DWG drawings + XLSX BOQ + MPP schedule.
- ✅ **CO4 and CO5** of PBCET604 are the primary outcomes assessed by this module.
- ✅ **Bloom's Level**: This module targets L3 (Apply) to L6 (Create) on the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Common Formulas to Memorize (One-Liners)

- Excavation: $V = L \times B \times D$
- Dry volume: $V_{dry} = 1.52 \times V_{wet}$
- Steel weight: $W_{steel} = V_{steel} \times 7850$
- CPM forward: $ES = \max(EF_{pred})$; $EF = ES + D$
- CPM backward: $LF = \min(LS_{succ})$; $LS = LF - D$
- Floats: $TF = LS - ES$; $FF = \min(ES_{succ}) - EF$
- Item cost: $C = Q \times R$
- Project cost: $C_{total} = C_{subtotal} + \text{Contingency} + \text{GST}$

### Exam-Day Checklist

- ☐ Read the question carefully and **underline the verbs** (compute, list, explain, draw).
- ☐ For numerical answers, **show all steps with units**; partial marking depends on it.
- ☐ Always **perform backward pass** to confirm the critical path.
- ☐ For BOQ, **include the wastage %** as a separate row.
- ☐ For diagrams, use **proper labels and scales**; use Mermaid/draw.io in practice.
- ☐ End with a **concluding remark** linking back to the project planning context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Students**: This PBL module is **integrative** — it combines drawing (graphics), estimation (mathematics), scheduling (CPM), and resources (management). Mastery requires **hands-on practice with AutoCAD + Excel + MS Project** on at least one real or hypothetical G+1 building project. The exam questions, although theoretical, test your ability to **apply** these tools to a problem. Treat each question as a **mini-PBL exercise**.
